## Summary
OF SHIPPING
Fifth Edition
by LANE C. KENDALL
London
CHAPMAN AND HALL For my sister
ELISABETH KENDALL THOMPSON
Fellow, American Institute of Architects
in appreciation of
her lifelongfriendship and unswerving support
in many times of crisis T
HIS VOL U M E has been written to describe the
business side of a commercial enterprise whose field is the entire
civilized world. Historically, the theory and knowledge of shipping
management, as distinguished from the practical skills of seaman
ship, have been transmitted from one generation to the next by
word of mouth.

## THE BUSINESS
OF SHIPPING
Fifth Edition
by LANE C. KENDALL
London
CHAPMAN AND HALL

For my sister
ELISABETH KENDALL THOMPSON
Fellow, American Institute of Architects
in appreciation of
her lifelongfriendship and unswerving support
in many times of crisis

## Preface
T
HIS VOL U M E has been written to describe the
business side of a commercial enterprise whose field is the entire
civilized world. Historically, the theory and knowledge of shipping
management, as distinguished from the practical skills of seaman
ship, have been transmitted from one generation to the next by
word of mouth. Little has been put on paper, primarily because the
finest exponents of the art of steamship management have been too
busy with their day-to-day concerns to do so. The "working level"
personnel often are superbly competent, but rarely qualify as liter
ary craftsmen.
It has been my aim, in preparing this analysis of the principles of
the "business" of commercial shipping, to describe that which trans
pires in the various divisions of a shipowning and operating organi
zation. Insofar as possible, the procedures followed in the offices
have been described and explained, as well as the underlying prin
ciples of management by which their decisions are reached.
In the process of learning the principles and practices that are
set forth in these pages, I have spent ajoy-filled lifetime in associa
tion with ships. It has been my good fortune to work in large and
small American steamship offices, to operate a major cargo termi
nal, to participate in establishing and putting into effect the policies
of a world-girdling American steamship organization, and to teach
young men these principles learned from experience as well as
from precept.
My years with ships and seamen have been filled with much toil
and many strains. They also have been crowded with pleasant
associations and fascinating experiences. I have discovered that
with increased knowledge of the endless ramifications of the steam
ship business my interest in each day's work has deepened and
grown more rewarding. May I hope that those who read this book

x THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
also will gain greater understanding of a worthwhile way oflife, and
thus heighten their appreciation of the achievements of each pass
ing day?
This volume is the fifth edition; the first appeared in 1973.
While much of the material found in the fourth edition has been
retained, a significant amount of new information has been added
in order to reflect new developments in the industry and to bring
the book up to date. It is my hope that the depth of detail and
analysis which characterize this present volume will prove to be of
value to the readers of these pages.
The names of those who have assisted me throughout the
decades of my association with the steamship industry are far too
numerous to list, even if all of them were known to me. Some who
have contributed importantly to my knowledge were nameless to
me, but I have not forgotten their generous sharing of experience
and hard-earned wisdom. Friends and correspondents in every
type of shipping office around the world have been more than
gracious in their willingness to contribute in some measure to my
professional edification. To them all, I extend my heartfelt thanks.
Any errors, of course, are my own, and I assume full
responsibility.
- Lane Carter Kendall

## Introduction
SH
I PS and the management of ships represent one of
man's oldest economic enterprises. There is no time in recorded
history when man was not using watercraft of some sort to provide
communication, and with communication inevitably came trade.
Trade began first between tribes, rapidly extending to nations over
routes that were pioneered before the dawn of history. During the
great Age of Exploration, the epochal voyages of Columbus, Ma
gellan, Vasco da Gama, and the illustrious company of "gentlemen
adventurers" established the principle that man could plan where
he would market his goods, and could build the ships needed to
transport those goods.
Today, approximately 25,000 ships of oceangoing size ply the
sea-routes. There are no seas that have not been traversed and
there are no shores untouched; there is no time of day or night
when scores of ships are not pushing their toilsome way across the
broad expanses of the oceans. There is, likewise, no hour when
someone ashore is not concerned with the welfare of the ships at
sea. The need to find cargoes and passengers; the requirement to
provide berthing space and representatives to assure that the ship is
attended promptly, or supplied with fuel, stores, and water; the
endless search for crew members to replace those unavoidable
casualties of the sea; and the problems of repairing damage suf
fered in the course of sea-travel-these are the responsibilities of
the owners, managers, and agents of commercial vessels.
The business of shipping, then, is a world-encircling enterprise
whose devotees must have a vast knowledge of matters great and
small, an enthusiasm for adventure even when it is vicarious and
experienced through the medium of telegrams and letters, and a
breadth of vision that stretches beyond the horizons of the seas. It is
a world removed in some degree from the ordinary routines of

## 4 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
commerce, for each ship becomes a personality; each voyage a
separate undertaking; each day a time of challenge; each event in
far-off lands of possible significance in the movement of the ships
across the waters. No other human activity is so important to so
many people, and no other enterprise is so subject to conditions
over which it has no control. To those whose lives are engaged in
the ramified operations of shipping, it is the most rewarding of
business ventures because there is the awareness that without ships,
life would lack much of its variety and vitality.
For those who heed the call of the sea; who face the rigors oflife
in constant conflict with the great forces of nature; who find the
romance of far places not just across the horizons but also in the
bales and boxes, the "black gold" pumped into vast tanks, and the
people who travel from land to land, there is a peculiar and deep
satisfaction. Their lives, anonymous though they may be to the
world, are devoted to the service of their fellows, to making the
world better, to creating new ideas, and to binding together the
affairs of humanity. These men-and increasingly these women-
<
are the professionals who find true happiness only when they are
"off soundings," away from the humdrum routine of life ashore.
They represent a special breed of heroic stature who ridicule any
notion of romance and laugh at the sweet words of the poet who
writes of far places and entrancing shores. They complain that they
never have a chance to make friends ashore, but return voyage
after voyage to the irresistible ships and the lure of the unknown
beyond the horizon. They serve, and that is their reason for living.
It is about this fascinating world of shipping, of international
commerce, of strange and exciting names and places, of unending
variety, as well as rigorous competition, that this book is written. Its
purpose is to lift the veil of mystery just enough to indicate why this
fascination exists; to introduce those who would learn about a
specialized world to the ideas and the thinking of this ancient yet
new and exciting dominion of the seas.

## CHAPTER 1 - Liner Service and Tramp Shipping
MERCHANT SHIPPING, considered from the
standpoint of types of service provided, may be divided into two
major categories: Liner Service and Tramp Shipping. While there are
some similarities, the differences in the theory and techniques of
management of these two types of marine transportation are no
table. The service rendered, the geographic area covered, the oper
ating problems, the relationship between vessel owner and vessel
user, and the actual employment of the ship, vary markedly be
tween the two categories. It is important, therefore, to be aware of
those areas where the management procedures are congruent; it is
equally significant that the differences be comprehended.
Liner Service Tramp Shipping

## CHAPTER 2 - Tramp Shipping
DURING the half century which ended in 1914, most
of the transoceanic cargoes of coal, ores, and grain were carried in
comparatively small, general-purpose cargo ships. Moving as they
did on routings which varied from voyage to voyage, hauling now
one commodity and then another, entering and leaving port with
out fanfare or publicity, and often in need of paint, these vessels
gained the appellation of "tramp steamers." To the extent that their
wanderings and unkempt appearance paralleled those of that fra
ternity of homeless men known as "tramps," the sobriquet may
have been appropriate. What was not apparent was the careful and
constant concern exercised over the ships by their owners, contest
ing for the available business with each other and with the shippers
of goods needing ocean transportation, and taking pride in their
ability to deliver the goods in accordance with contracts drawn up
with consummate skill and negotiated with great vigor.
The purpose of tramp ships may be stated quite simply: to
provide the efficient, convenient, timely, and economic transporta
tion required by the many kinds of goods needed for a complex,
industrialized society. Converting this declaration of purpose into
the reality of cross-ocean movement of millions of tons of com
modities typified by ores, coal, grain, and fertilizers is a task requir
ing enormous skill and a multitude of resources. Underlying the
dedicated effort of the people afloat and ashore who comprise the
tramp shipping industry is the necessity to meet constantly chang
ing demands, and therefore to find the ways and means of assuring
the flexibility of ship operation which is one of the principal reasons
for the existence of the tramp.
I wish to acknowledge my debt to Mr. J. Bes, of Hilversum, Netherlands, author of
Chartering and Shipping Terms, for his invaluable assistance in the preparation of the
three chapters on tramp shipping.

## TRAMP SHIPPING 13
A modern general-purpose tramp of 26,592 tons deadweight, the 15-
knot Mario C. L. was built in 1974. Photo: Jeff Blinn, courtesy Moran
Towing Co.
It is an acknowledged fact of both geography and economics
that the raw materials so essential to the life of industrialized na
tions rarely are located close to the places where they are needed.
Chrome ore is vital as an ingredient in high-grade steel, but it is
found in large quantities only in comparatively underdeveloped
areas. The availability of inexpensive and dependable transoceanic
transportation is of crucial importance to both producing and
consuming regions. More dramatic, perhaps, because of the great
er volatility of demand, is the growth and distribution of grain.
From the vast and fertile plains of the United States, Canada,
Argentina, and Australia comes a never-ending stream of the gold
en staple. For generations, there has been a steady and predictable
movement of grain from these four producing regions to England,
Northern Europe, and the Mediterranean nations. Irregularly,
depending upon climatic and meteorological conditions beyond
the control of man, there have been urgent appeals from India,
China, Russia, and those areas of Africa where drought and over
grazing have combined to make very real the terrifying threat of
famine. Tramp ships fit into both delivery patterns, providing the
means of transportation in the established trades and, whenever

## 14 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
required, shifting to new routes to alleviate critical shortages
wherever they may occur. Let a crop fail in Argentina, however,
and the distribution of ships reflects immediately the reduction in
the cargoes available for export. Should the unlikely combination
of a crop failure in Argentina coincide with a sharply curtailed
harvest in China, the rearrangement of tramp shipping routes to
meet the new requirements for transportation would be on a world
wide basis.
This flexibility in adapting to unanticipated and major changes
in patterns of shipping has made the tramping industry dispro
portionately significant in the economic life of the world. No other
type of shipping service can claim to be so sensitive to demand. The
very nature of tramp shipping is to seek cargoes where they may be
found, rather than to operate on a fixed route with a rigid schedule
of departures and arrivals regardless of the quantity of goods which
may be offered for carriage.
Another reason for the continued existence of tramp shipping
lies in the fact that it provides the indispensable over-ocean trans
portation at the lowest possible cost. Metallic ores, grains, forest
products, fertilizers, and related commodities are carried on sur
prisingly long voyages for freights which often aggregate only
one-tenth to one-third of the sales prices of the goods. A measure of
what alternative modes of transport might charge was revealed by a
study made in 1975 which showed that the expense of sea-carriage
was about one percent of the cost of moving the same item the same
distance by air, five percent of the charge to haul by motortruck,
and ten percent of the railroad freight bill. This same study also
brought out that the principal routes and average length of voyage
for the major tramp cargoes were:
Iron ore: Australia to Japan; Australia to Europe; Scandinavia to
Northwest Europe; West Africa to Europe; Brazil to Japan; West
Coast of South America to Japan; Indian Ocean ports to Japan;
Indian Ocean ports to Europe; and Venezuela to the United States.
The average voyage was 4,800 nautical miles in length.
Grain: United States and Canada to Europe; United States and
Canada to the Far East; United States and Canada to the Soviet
Union; Argentina to Europe; Argentina to the Far East; and Aus
tralia to the Far East. The length of the average voyage was 5,150
nautical miles.

## TRAMP SHIPPING 15
Coal: United States to Japan; Australia to Japan; United States
to Europe; Canada to Japan. There also was significant movement
within the European area. The average voyage extended over
4,700 nautical miles.
Forest Products (including logs, sawn lumber, wood-pulp, and pa
per): North America to Japan; Northwest Coast of the United States
and British Columbia to Gulf of Mexico and Adantic ports, or to Eu
rope; Scandinavia to Northwest Europe; Southeast Asia to Japan.
Voyages on the average extended to about 2,700 nautical miles.
To generate the total of over three trillion ton-miles of ocean
transportation which the movement of these commodities re
quired, on January 1, 1983, there was a fleet of 19,495 ships. Based
on an analysis of shipping statistics completed in 1975, the dead
weight of the typical tramp steamer would have been not more than
20,000 tons. Using this criterion, approximately one third of the
14,280 vessels classified as "freighters" would have been operated
in the tramp trades, supplementing the 5,215 ships designated as
bulk carriers. These carriers' capacities would have ranged from
20,000 tons to over 250,000 tons deadweight.
In a rather technical sense, the ships exceeding 20,000 gross
tons should not be considered as true tramps because they were
designed, built, and operated to carry only ores, or coal, or grain.
Many of these large ships-their average deadweight was 70,000
tons-were committed to haul one commodity exclusively, and
might have spent a major portion of their operational lives in this
one trade.l An unusual example of combination ships being used in
different trades as a routine matter is seen in the case of Wallen ius
Lines, of Sweden, which took delivery in 1973 and 1974 of the Aida
and Otello, each of approximately 51,500 tons deadweight. Built in
Gdynia, Poland, these vessels were designed to carry simulta
neously 45,000 tons of iron ore in four holds (Nos. 1,3,5, and 7)
and about 3,500 compact-sized automobiles in the other four holds.
These "garage holds" were built with thirteen decks. Normal em
ployment of these two ships was from Japan to Europe with J apan
ese cars, Europe to North America with European cars, and North
America to Far East and Japan carrying coal or iron ore.2 The
quantity of ore, coal, and grain which must be delivered to ports too
shallow to accommodate the giants assures that the modern gen
eral-purpose tramp of 14,000 to 20,000 tons and the bulkers of

## 16 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
between 35,000 and 55,000 tons deadweight capacity will remain
important units in the world's fleets.
Tramp ships, to repeat, contribute to the economic life of the
world in an absolutely indispensable manner and, as has been
noted, do so at low cost. There are times when the freight rate, as
compared to the value of the goods transported, actually is lower
than the ten to thirty percent figure previously quoted, while occa
sionally and inevitably the proportion is greater. The explanation
for these variations lies in the fact of competition. When circum
stances warrant, freight rates decline, as when there is a glut of
wheat worldwide. The need for transportation is diminished, and
the demand for ships is reduced correspondingly. As in any mar
ketplace where the laws of supply and demand prevail, cutthroat
competition among the suppliers commences in the attempt to find
charters, and rates drop swiftly. Contrariwise, if there should be a
major crop failure (as there was in the Soviet Union in 1974),
enormous shipments of wheat from many producing areas sud
denly will be required to replace that which could not be harvested.
The demand for vessels exceeds the number available, and freight
rates climb precipitately.
The example of the unexpected crop failure and the resultant
effect upon the charter market focuses attention on one of the
more interesting and significant aspects of the tramp shipping
industry. Shipowners engaged in this trade constantly search for
opportunities to capitalize upon situations similar to that described.
When these enterprising individuals learn of such events, usually
through their ramified intelligence system of brokers, agents, and
representatives worldwide, to say nothing of their own experience
in gauging the market day by day, they react promptly and deci
sively. Orders are dispatched to vessels not otherwise committed to
make their way to the critical area, and negotiations with potential
charterers are initiated. If the demand for transportation is very
great, the owner whose ships have the greatest speed will stand to
reap the maximum rewards.
It is, however, a paradox of the tramping business that the very
action of these audacious capitalists, who will risk a multi-thousand
mile voyage on the chance of winning a single lucrative cargo, is the
guarantor that the high freight rates will be reduced substantially in
a relatively short period. Only so long as there is a shortage of ships

## TRAMP SHIPPING 17
for the requirements of the moment will the rates be inflated; as the
concentration of vessels in the exporting area increases, the scarcity
is alleviated and freight rates plummet. All too often, instead of the
gamble paying a premium to the speculator, it becomes necessary
for the owner-speculator to accept substandard freight rates as a
means of reducing the losses incident to working the ship to other
ports where conditions seem more favorable.
Tramp shipping not only is extremely flexible in responding to
the demands of commerce but also is very sensitive to changes in the
patterns of sea transportation. These characteristics may be ex
plained in part by the fact that the world fleet of several thousand
tramp vessels is owned by persons of at least a score of nationalities.
Domiciled in many different parts of the world, these owners keep
in touch with the demands of commerce through a complex and
fascinating network of representatives which spreads throughout
the world. Included in the huge fleet are dozens of handsome, well
maintained, expensive new ships which are large, fast, and very
efficient. There are also many smaller, slower, less productive ves
sels significantly past their prime which remain active only so long
as shortages of carriers make it possible for them to find employ
ment. This fleet is scattered over the fabled seven seas, with typical
craft in almost every port of the world.
Many ships of the tramp fleet belong to individuals. In some
cases, one person may own as many as twenty, but it is more usual to
find "fleets" of two or three or four units. This is especially true
among Greek owners, whose loyalty to family shipping enterprises
is a distinguishing characteristic. These small family groups are
noted for their sturdy independence; few of them have affiliated
with the larger consortia of tramp operators.
There also are a few great shipowning enterprises which in
clude within their corporate structures tramp shipping activities.
This comes about for several reasons, one of which is to participate
in the profits to be earned. Another reason is to develop new trade
routes, an interesting exercise in which tramp ships explore the
true potential of business. If charters are to be had again and yet
again between a range of ports in one region (as, for example, the
United States north of Cape Hatteras but not east of New York) and
another area such as the countries at the eastern end of the Medi
terranean, carrying substantially the same cargoes trip after trip,

## 18 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
the possibility of regular liner service deserves to be probed with
ships actually competing for this type of business. Often it is found
that the new liner route is both desirable and economically feasible.
An additional, but considerably less compelling, reason for owning
tramps is to assure that vessels which become surplus to the liner
service can be kept sailing, even when not returning a profit, at no
greater cost than laying them up in idleness. Liner companies also
may find it advantageous to have a number of ships engaged in
tramping because, as temporary shortages in liner-type tonnage
develop, vessels may be transferred from tramp to liner operations
by a simple company directive. Only those tramps with the speed,
hull design, and installed cargo-handling equipment needed in
liner service can be used in this manner. There also is the possibility
for these large corporations to phase out slightly obsolete ships
from their liner fleets by transferring them to the tramp segment
for the last years of their operational lives.
These reasons have lost much of their force with the advent of
containerization in the major liner services. Whereas in the days of
what is now described as "conventional break-bulk operations," the
structural differences between liners and tramps were not so great
as to make them incompatible, this no longer is true. Ships carrying
full cargoes of containers must be built for that purpose, and
cannot be switched into tramp trading for a while and then re
turned to liner operations. Consequently, few large steamship com
panies today have subsidiaries to operate tramp fleets.
It is an interesting fact of economic history that tramp shipping,
as it is known today, did not come into being until the second half of
the nineteenth century. By this time, steam had been harnessed to
machines, and the Industrial Revolution was in full swing. The
demand for raw materials to supply the machines had begun to be
felt worldwide. Cotton and wool for the textile industry of England
were brought from distant shores, and to those points were re
turned the manufactured goods of English mills and factories.
During these same years of major change, steam power was adapt
ed to ships. The insatiable demand for coal to fire their relatively
primitive, low-pressure boilers necessitated the establishment of
refueling points which were not too far apart geographically, but
which did have some commercial activity or promise of develop-

## TRAMP SHIPPING 19
ment. These coaling stations grew in number and, as trade routes
expanded, were located farther and farther from England. A brisk
export business in "best Welsh coals" evolved rather quickly and
made for a satisfactory balance in ship operation, especially when
the coaling stations were comparatively close to those ports where
return cargoes could be obtained. The sturdy but unglamourous
tramps brought back to England whatever they could find, be it
cotton or wool or grain or ore. Always assured of outbound cargoes
of coal should there be no requirement for their services to haul
loads of manufactured goods to the distant market places, the
tramps soon were the visible reminders of the vast reach of the
British empire.
Admittedly, the steamships did cost more to operate than the
wind-driven sailing ships, but their increased reliability and speed,
and particularly the possibility of predicting within a few days the
exact time of arrival of a cargo, erased that pecuniary disadvantage.
Furthermore, the decreased time needed to accomplish a voyage,
as compared to the sailing ship, meant that a single steamship could
carry more tonnage during a year than could its more beautiful
rival. If proof of the economy of the coal-fired steamer's great
speed were needed, this was provided by performance. In a given
period, the faster, engine-powered vessel could deliver more goods
than the sailing ship, and so created greater carrying capacity
without the need of building and operating more ships. Finally, the
opening of the Suez Canal in 1869 sounded the death knell of the
sailing ship because only powered vessels could negotiate the Red
Sea on any commercially acceptable schedule.
England was the great industrial center of the Western Euro
pean area, and the combination of her need for raw materials, her
exports of manufactured goods, and the constant flow of "best
Welsh coals" to outlying coaling stations resulted in the require
ment for a large tramp fleet. Just as English capital had responded
to the opportunities ashore which had been afforded by the Indus
trial Revolution, so English financiers in the last quarter of the
nineteenth century saw the chance for rich profits in shipping. By
1900, half of the oceangoing steamship tonnage of the world was
under British registry. While this numerical superiority gradually
was lost as other national fleets were expanded, it remained true at

## 20 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
least until 1920 that more ships flew the Union Jack-familiarly
known in maritime circles as the "Red Duster"-than any other
flag.
In 1900, the best and most modern tramp steamer, which was
not necessarily the typical ship, had a cargo capacity of about seven
thousand tons. When operated with a clean bottom and in fair
weather (Force 2 on the Beaufort scale of winds), she had a service
speed of eleven knots. As described by the novelist William McFee,
who served for years as an engineer in such a ship, the hull was
110.0 meters (370 feet) long, 14.4 meters (48 feet) in the beam, and
had a full, rounded bow which facilitated stowage of cargo at the
expense of speed. There were three boilers which collectively
burned twenty-five tons of "best Welsh coals" each day as they
supplied steam to the triple expansion reciprocating engine of
1,800 indicated horsepower. One set of cargo booms was installed
over each of the four or five hatches.
A ship of this type would expect to find regular employment
carrying coal outward from England, and would return in due
course with a cargo of ore in bulk or cotton in bales or sugar in bags.
A year's voyages would earn a profit of twelve percent for her
owners, a figure supported by the fact that her acquisition cost was
£42,000. This cost must be considered in the light of contemporary
values. The full cargo of coal was worth about £2,000. Crew wages
were comparably low: the master was paid £25 a month; the chief
engineer earned £20, while the senior mate received £1 O. A junior
engineer worked all month for £6.3
From 1900 to 1914, the size of general-purpose dry cargo ships
was increased slightly to a maximum of 7,500 tons deadweight
capacity. Greater engineering efficiency reduced the consumption
of coal while improving average speeds to approximately ten knots.
Until the end of World War I, coal remained the predominant fuel.
Between 1919 and 1939, these ships were enlarged to about ten
thousand tons deadweight, and oil fuel, both for boilers and diesel
engines, began to achieve popularity.
The titanic ship construction program undertaken by the Unit
ed States during World War II produced thousands of ships of a
single standardized design. The Liberty ship was sold worldwide
beginning in 1946, and quickly became the universal general-pur
pose tramp. Unlike the earlier ships of this class, the Liberty had a

## TRAMP SHIPPING 21
single 'tween deck, which enhanced her adaptability for both bulk
and packaged cargoes.4 Until they were twenty years old (1962 and
thereafter), Liberties were used on every international trade route.
It was about 1950 that dry-cargo ship sizes began to grow
significantly. As the ravages of war were repaired, and European
shipyards returned to full productivity, the requirements of sea
borne commerce were met with new construction. Hulls were
lengthened, widened, and deepened, and cargo deadweights in
creased to about 14,000 or 15,000 tons. In 1968, a British shipyard,
Austin & Pickers gill, introduced its replacement for the aging Lib
erties. The S.D. 14 (derived from the technical description of "Shel
ter Decker, 14,000 tons deadweight") gained widespread accept
ance, and by 1981 a total of 204 ships of this design had been built,
making this vessel the most successful series produced ship in
peacetime history.5
While the general-purpose tramp was growing, the bulk carrier
had been developed into a specialized vehicle for the transportation
of coal, grains, and ores. Evolving from the distinctive iron ore
carriers which had been in existence since early in the century, the
new bulk carrier was intended to haul with equal efficiency and
economy anyone of those three commodities, all of which moved in
enormous quantities. The first of these multipurpose bulkers could
lift 40,000 tons, and demonstrated conclusively the economy of
great scale. In 1981, bulkers up to 280,000 tons deadweight were in
routine service on many trade routes.
Excellent reasons underlay this trend toward specialization. A
vessel designed to transport a particular commodity will incorpo
rate those features which make her ideal for that trade: overall size,
draft, ratio of cargo stowage space to deadweight tonnage, dimen
sions of hatch openings, type of cargo-handling gear, speed, and
economy of operation, to name some of the most important. For
hauling the intended commodity, the specialized ship should be the
most efficient and convenient that can be built. So long as the trade
in that commodity provides regular employment for the vessel, she
will bring benefits to her owners because of her efficiency, and will
gratify her charterers because of the lower freight rates and the
faster delivery of cargoes she makes possible. The entire commer
cial community will gain because she permits the most efficacious
use of capital.

## 22 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Although they are restricted in their choice of cargoes, these
specialized carriers often, if not predominantly, are operated as
conventional tramp ships-which is to say, they are offered for time
or voyage charter on the world market, they seek out cargoes and
carry them where it suits the convenience of the charterer to have
his goods discharged, and they compete both among themselves
and with the general-purpose tramps for what business there may
be. Because of their great size, however, they are limited as to the
ports they can enter, but this is only a minor problem for them.
The emergence in recent years of these huge specialized car
riers has intensified the normal struggle for employment on the
part of the conventional general-purpose tramp, but has not elimi
nated that ship from the maritime scene. It still is true that the
general-purpose tramp, unlike its big competitor, is able to enter
most ports of any commercial significance, and can be berthed at
existing port facilities. Furthermore, the smaller size of the general
purpose vessel, as compared with the large specialized carrier, may
serve the needs of a charterer very well indeed.
A manufacturer of fertilizer, for example, normally processes
15,000 tons of phosphate rock each week, and has storage facilities
for a maximum of 40,000 tons of this material. By chartering
several I5,000-ton tramps to establish and maintain a schedule to
deliver one shipload of phosphate rock approximately every seven
to ten days, the manufacturer keeps his current supplies at a satis
factory level, and has ample reserves if the ships are delayed for no
more than two weeks. In effect, the ships have been integrated into
the manufacturer's supply system at the cost of the slightly higher
freight rate which they charge as compared to that of the larger
bulkers.
It is almost axiomatic that those shippers and receivers of goods
who have the physical facilities to handle and to dispose of immense
quantities of a particular cargo, and the financial resources to
handle transactions of great magnitude, will demand that they be
furnished carriers which will give them the economies of large
scale. The big bulk carriers have been the logical response to these
demands and, within the limitations on their employment imposed
by their length and draft, provide the required transportation at
the lowest possible cost.
This type of ship enjoys a well earned and important place in

## TRAMP SHIPPING 23
Top: A modern tramp with unusual gantry cranes, the Bulk Eagle is
well-adapted to handle commodities like coal and iron ore. Bottom: Ship
board gantry cranes are paired to handle extra-long items. Photos:
Munck International.
oceanborne commerce. It meets a defined need, and has demon
strated the soundness of the principle that the larger the vessel, the
lower the unit cost of carriage. Only those organizations which are
geared to handle, both physically and financially, enormous quanti
ties of specific commodities are able to take advantage of the bene
fits which accrue from chartering these big ships.
The modern general-purpose tramp, on the other hand, is

## 24 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
intended to serve, and does so very successfully, a number of
different trades. In one year, a fleet of four of these tramps lifted
cargoes of pig iron, black iron, wheat middling pellets, scrap iron,
logs, timber, tapioca, wood chips, poonac (coconut oil cake), ilmen
ite sand, iron ore, grain, coal, bauxite, and cement.6 Designed to
operate into and out of ports which have relatively shallow draft
and only a minimum of port facilities typified by wharves, piers, and
transit sheds with their supporting upland areas, these vessels are
equipped with sufficient booms, derricks, or cranes to make them
"self-sustaining," i.e., capable of being loaded and unloaded by use
of their own gear. Finally, the carrying capacity of the general
purpose tramp is established at least in part with reference to the
quantities of whatever is offered for transportation in the different
trades. Often this level of business matches exactly the capability of
the ship. It is never a simple coincidence.
It is axiomatic that the controlling depth of water and the
peculiarities of the channels in a port fix the limits of ship sizes
which can be accommodated in a given harbor. If a ship drawing
10.5 meters (35 feet) of water, fully laden at summer load line, is
offered for a trip to a harbor with a minimum depth of 9.6 meters
(32 feet), there are two alternatives open to the potential charterer.
One is to reject the offered ship because she is too large. The other
is to accept the ship, load her only to the minimum depth, and make
the best of the transaction. Circumstances prevailing in the freight
market at the time when a decision must be reached will determine
the alternative that is chosen.
Accommodations for the personnel staffing tramp ships have
been improved steadily through the years. At the turn of the
century, most of the marine superintendents ashore as well as the
licensed officers aboard the tramps had served as apprentices in
sailing vessels, and expected no more comfort in the steamers than
they had found in the windjammers. Quarters were stark and
utilitarian. Novelist William McFee, a veteran of years of service in
British-flag tramps in pre-1914 days, described the conditions he
encountered: small rooms allotted to licensed personnel about as
comfortable as cells in a penitentiary, no electric lights, no heat, no
running water, and no bathing facilities.7 All this has changed, and
today living arrangements for all crew members are excellent. At
the same time, much more attention is given to assure that the food

## TRAMP SHIPPING 25
is well prepared and served in attractive surroundings.
Competition, however, remains unaltered in its harshness. The
law of supply and demand determines which ships earn a profit. If
there are many cargoes and few bottoms, there is rivalry among
shippers and the freight rates climb. When the opposite condition
prevails, and too many ships are chasing too few cargoes, the
shipowners fight among themselves to obtain what they can, often
accepting business at suicidally low rates-which explains the old
saying in the tramp trade that "one good year is balanced by seven
bad ones."
Until 1966, when international transportation of cargoes load
ed in containers began, tramps engaged in the foreign trade of the
United States vied with the cargo liners for bulk cargoes such as
grain, lumber, and some types of ore. The liners would seek "par
cels" of three to six thousand tons to fill otherwise unused space,
and would quote freight rates that were barely adequate to cover
the cost of stevedoring. This practice hurt the tramps because they
had to accept these same low rates for full loads of eight to ten
thousand tons. The virtual disappearance of the break-bulk cargo
liner on most of these trade routes in many instances has been
accompanied by the entry of the specialized large bulkers, thus
perpetuating the competition with the general-purpose tramp.
Depreciation-the annual provision made by a prudent owner
to write off the value of the units of his fleet-is based usually on a
theoretical twenty-year life for a ship. In the tramp trade, however,
earnings are not so consistent that it is feasible each year to put into
the depreciation account even five percent of the purchase price, as
good financial practice might suggest. Some owners strive to cut the
book value of new ships by as much as they can afford during the
first two or three years of ownership, and thereafter as earnings
permit. The sooner a ship's value is written off, the more quickly
may the owner compete for cargoes in the bad years. Even if it
should take a full twenty years to amortize the purchase of a vessel,
it does not follow automatically that she will be replaced. So long as
cargoes can be found, it is likely that the old vessel will be continued
. .
In serVIce.
Competition for cargoes, slower speeds than those of the cargo
liners, and non repetitive operations combine to restrict the tramp
owner to a particular segment of commercial activity. In practice,

## 26 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
he transports, usually on a resupply and stockpile basis, materials
typified by cement, coal, grain, fertilizers, lumber, and ores of
various kinds. The unit sales price of these commodities is small;
the purchase price at the loading port is low, and the difference
between purchase price and sales price does not permit very high
freight rates. This becomes obvious when a given item is available
from two widely separated geographical regions. If that which
originates at the more distant point cannot be sold in the same
marketplace for no more than the price of the goods from the
nearby source, the probability is that there will be no sale. Despite
this pressure to keep freight rates low, it can happen in some
instances that the cost of transportation equals the value of the raw
material, with the predictable result that the market for that item is
restricted.
To succeed in the tramping enterprise requires that the opera
tor know something about practically all the raw materials moving
in the world's commerce. At the same time, he must be acquainted
with the physical characteristics of, and the cargo-handling equip
ment available in, those places where these materials are loaded and
discharged. Always, too, he must be the optimistic speculator.
In addition to the personal attributes, the owner of tramp ships
must have access to a dependable system which maintains contact
with potential charterers anywhere in the world. It would be pro
hibitively expensive as well as administratively impractical to at
tempt to maintain an office in every port, or to arrange for perma
nent representation in those cities. To meet this demand for a low
cost, worldwide agency which will provide commercial intelligence
and advice when it is needed, the institution of the ship broker
evolved through the centuries. The members of this branch of the
shipping fraternity are the specialists who find cargoes for ships
and ships for cargoes.
The center of this fascinating activity is in London, and the
headquarters are located in the Baltic Exchange, not far from the
Thames river and famous Tower Bridge. Here at the "Baltic,"
brokers who have been elected individually to membership on the
basis of their personal integrity, knowledge, and resourcefulness,
meet to work out charters for their principals. One man represents
a shipowner and another stands in the place of a shipper. For
example, suppose that Angus MacAndrew, of Glasgow, is seeking a

## TRAMP SHIPPING 27
load for his ship, the Highlander, now at anchor in Hampton Roads.
MacAndrew notifies John Lawson, his broker in London, who
consults other brokers on the floor of the Baltic. In short order, he
learns from Basil Smith that Li Wong in Shanghai needs a ship to
carry grain to China from either an Atlantic or a Gulf Coast port of
the United States. Following brief discussion between Lawson and
Smith, an agreement as to price is reached and, after approval from
the principals, the contract is made. Before the sun sets in London,
the Highlander has been notified of her next employment and is on
her way to the loading port.
The advantage of the Baltic is that here are to be found daily the
representatives of practically all the owners of tramp ships in the
world as well as brokers whose principals are seeking tonnage for
their goods. It is much more expeditious to obtain the information
and then to negotiate a charter on the floor of the Baltic than to
canvass the brokers' offices by telephone, as is done in New York.
Once a vessel is offered on option to a potential charterer, that ship
is unavailable to other bidders, no matter how attractive their offers
may be. Until the option is exercised or expires, the broker is bound
by his word that the offerer will be protected. Only after the
transaction has been completed is the contract drawn up. To pre
vent confusion, especially when international telephone conversa
tions are involved, it is common practice to confirm offers and
counteroffers by cable or telex, but these written messages do not
replace the spoken pledges made by the brokers.
Shipowners as well as charterers must be aware of what charter
hire rates are being offered or accepted, in precisely the same way
that traders in stocks and bonds must keep abreast of swings in the
securities markets. Reports of vessel charters, or "fixtures," are
published in shipping journals in a technical language which con
veys the essentials of the transactions in a minimum of words. The
fixture of the Highlander, for example, would be publicized in a
paragraph like this:
U. S. Northern Range to coast of China. Motorship Highlander, 27,000
tons, five percent, heavy grain, $36.50; option two ports China
$37.50; 60¢ extra for U. S. Gulfloading option; FlOT; Feb. 1-12.
Interpreted for the layman, this reports that the motorship
Highlander has been chartered to carry about 27,000 tons of heavy

## 28 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
grain, plus or minus five percent at the master's discretion to obtain
a seaworthy load and still store and fuel his ship adequately without
losing too much revenue. The grain will be loaded in a port between
Cape Hatteras and Portland, Maine, and transported to the coast of
China at a rate of $36.50 for each ton loaded. If the charterer elects
to discharge the cargo in two ports, the rate will be increased to
$37.50 per ton. Should the ship have to proceed to a United States
port on the Gulf of Mexico to take on her cargo, the charterer will
pay 60¢ additional per ton. FIOT (free in and out and trimmed)
means that all expenses connected with loading, discharging, and
trimming are for the account of the charterer. The Highlander must
report at the designated loading port not earlier than February 1
and not later than February 12.
In the case described above, the Highlander has been obtained
on a basis which suits the convenience of only one person, the
shipper. It is much the same as hiring a truck to haul a load of
goods, rather than sending the goods by railroad train. The truck
will proceed to the pickup point set by the shipper, and will arrive at
an hour arranged between truckman and shipper. It will be routed
either as directed by the shipper or by the driver using the shortest
route. If a railroad train is used, the goods will move over the tracks
on a fixed schedule, and will be picked up and delivered according
to the railroad's practice. The tramp is like the truck, whereas the
seagoing counterpart to the railroad train is the cargo liner, which
sails between advertised ports on a relatively inflexible schedule to
which the user must conform.
How does the shipper go about obtaining the exclusive use of a
tramp vessel? What arrangements are made to safeguard the in
terests of the shipowner? What guarantee does the shipper have
that the vessel will do what is required? What sorts of agreements
are made? The answers to these and related questions constitute
the essence of the chartering business, and are based upon the
maritime and commercial experience of thousands of men of past
generations as well as of the present time.
The written agreement setting forth the terms and conditions
under which the vessel owner makes a ship available to the shipper
on an exclusive basis is a specialized form of contract known in
maritime circles as a "charter-party," a name derived from the
Latin words, charta partita, which mean a paper divided so that each
signatory may keep an identical copy.8

## TRAMP SHIPPING 29
Charter-parties are diversified into three principal categories:
voyage, time, and bareboat.
The voyage charter is the most frequently employed because it
places the lightest burden upon the charterer. In essence, it is a
contract of affreightment covering the movement of a particular
cargo from one designated area to another, at a stipulated rate of
compensation (charter hire) for each ton of the goods actually
transported. The shipowner is responsible for all details of vessel
operation. The charterer, in addition to putting the cargo within
reach of the loading gear (which may be the ship's equipment or
dock-side cranes or chutes for ores and grains), must arrange for
the berthing places at ports of loading and discharge, and quite
often also must contract with stevedores to handle the cargo. Pay
ment for the ship's services must be made in accordance with the
provisions ofthe charter-party.
The time charter, while somewhat similar to the voyage charter
in keeping the shipowner responsible for operation of the vessel,
differs primarily in that the charterer obtains the use of the ship for
a period of time to carry cargoes not barred by the contract any
where within the geographical limitations set forth in the charter
party.
Under the terms of the bareboat (or demise) charter, the owner
transfers operational control of the ship to the charterer. The vessel
must be seaworthy and fully equipped at the time she is delivered to
the charterer, but the burden and responsibility of operation are
assumed by the charterer. Except for approving the nomination of
master and chief engineer, the owner does not influence the selec
tion of officers and crew. The charterer must purchase fuel, stores,
and food, pay all maintenance and drydocking expenses, and be
come the de facto manager of the vessel. The owner is kept in
formed of the movements of the ship, and periodically receives the
agreed payment which, as in the time charter, is based upon the
deadweight tonnage of the ship.
Both time and bareboat charters normally contain certain re
strictions concerning the types of cargo which may be carried. At
the minimum, the stipulation is that "any lawful merchandise not
injurious to the vessel" may be put aboard. Some contracts are more
specific; one document states that "No live stock [or] sulphur and
pitch in bulk to be shipped." Should the owner wish to protect his
ship from damage by certain goods, an "exclusionary clause" would

## 30 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
be inserted to limit the quantity of such goods to be transported. A
typical clause reads, "Injurious, inflammable, or dangerous goods
(such as acids, explosives, calcium carbide, ferro-silicon, naphtha,
motor spirit, tar, or any of their products) to be limited" to an
agreed maximum, and such dangerous goods must be "packed,
loaded, stowed, and discharged in accordance with the regulations"
of appropriate authorities. If these regulations entail any expenses,
including the cost of erecting and dismantling magazines, those
expenses shall be for the account of the charterers.
The time or bareboat charterer directs where the ship shall sail.
Because marine underwriters are very definite as to the areas of the
world where they will accept responsibility for damage sustained by
ships they insure, the time and bareboat charters contain a stipula
tion generally to the effect that "the charterers are to give the
necessary sailing instructions, subject to the limit of the charter, but
always within the trading limits set by the hull underwriters." The
newest forms of preprinted time charters contain a special blank
space in which the limits of the vessel's voyages are set forth with the
greatest care. A general stipulation is that the charterer shall not
order the ship to a port that is hazardous to health by reason of
fever or epidemic. A further restriction is imposed against sending
the ship into places where there is danger of being frozen in for the
duration of the winter. A typical "ice clause" follows this pattern:
The Vessel is not to be ordered to nor bound to enter any ice-bound
place or any place where lights, lightships, marks and buoys are or are
likely to be withdrawn by reason of ice on the Vessel's arrival or where
there is risk that ordinarily the Vessel will not be able on account of ice
to reach the place or to get out after having completed loading or
discharging. The Vessel is not to be obliged to force ice, nor to follow
icebreakers when inward bound. If on account of ice the Master
considers it dangerous to remain at the loading or discharging place
for fear of the Vessel being frozen in and/or damaged, he has liberty
to sail to a convenient open place and await the Charterers' fresh
instructions. Detention through any of the above causes to be for the
Charterers' account.
If the contemplated employment of the ship entails voyages to
ports where these conditions may be encountered, the terms and
conditions under which that activity will be performed must be
negotiated and set forth in minute detail in the charter-party.

## TRAMP SHIPPING 31
The owner invoked the "ice clause" when his ice-strengthened bulk car
rier encountered these conditions. Photo: A/S Hydraulik Brattvaag.
Any charterer who takes ships for a period of time becomes
involved, as has been indicated, in considerations related to the
expenditure of large sums of money (as for fuel) or the actual
scheduling of voyages and the unavoidable responsibility for loss of
time resulting from impaired performance of the ship. To protect
the charterer in both these typical instances, there must be agree
ment before the contract is signed as to the amount of fuel the ship's
power plant will consume in twenty-four hours at normal service
speed in calm seas, and this statement must be contained in the
charter-party. The speed of the ship and her carrying capacity,
expressed both in cubic feet of enclosed space and in deadweight
tons, are stipulated by the owner, along with numerous other
details of the ship's physical characteristics, in what are called the
"warranties." These are statements of fact which are expected to be
borne out by the performance of the vessel. Failure to live up to the
warranties can make the owner liable for breach of contract.

## 32 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Most of the warranties are provable without difficulty, but
speed and fuel consumption are affected noticeably by weather
conditions. Should a dispute arise over either or both of these
stipulations, there will be discussions between the contracting par
ties until a satisfactory adjustment is made. Should no compromise
be reached in these areas or in any other part of the charter-party,
the dispute may be referred to arbitrators. Their decision will have
the same force as a judgment handed down by a court of law.
The charterer always stipulates the port in which the ship is to
be made available to him-or, in the terms of the trade, "tendered"
by the owner. The charterer also sets forth the beginning and the
ending of the period of days during which tender (or delivery) of
the ship may be made. This period is known as "lay days." The
charterer also states the date beyond which he reserves the right to
cancel the agreement and to refuse to accept the tender of the
vessel. The owner protects himself from arbitrary action by includ
ing this clause in the charter-party:
I f the vessel cannot be delivered by the cancelling date, the Charter
ers, if required [by the owners], are to declare within 48 hours (Sun
days and holidays excluded) after receiving notice thereof whether
they cancel or will take delivery of the Vessel.
Should the contract be for a single voyage, the exact commodity
to be carried and the charter hire payable per ton of that commod
ity actually loaded, are designated, as are the ports of loading and
discharge. These details are important to the owner not only for the
obvious reasons but also because they permit him to look ahead for
other employment following the completion of the present charter.
Experience and knowledge will guide him in estimating the num
ber of days the ship will need to carry out the contract; a judicious
guess must be made as to the possible delays, and long-range
commitments thereafter can be consummated. During those peri
ods when there is reasonably good demand for ships, an astute
owner may be able to keep his vessel profitably employed for a full
year with hardly any movements in ballast.
Among provisions of the voyage charter are the number of days
allowed for loading and discharge (also known as "lay days")9, the
penalty for holding the ship in port beyond the allowed period for
working cargo ("demurrage"), and the premium for reducing the

## TRAMP SHIPPING 33
time spent in port ("dispatch"). For instance, a 20,000-ton tanker is
fixed for a load of petroleum from a Caribbean port to a range of
ports on the Atlantic coast of the United States north of Cape
Hatteras (generally referred to by the initials USNH); alternatively,
it may be assigned specific ports such as Aruba and New York. The
ship is allowed a certain number of hours for loading and discharg
ing; demurrage is set at $2,200 per day and dispatch at half that
rate, prorated to the actual hours.
Because the voyage charter imposes the least operational re
sponsibility upon the charterer and is convenient for the movement
of bulk commodities, it has become the most common form of
contract. The owner of the ship, under the "gross" form, pays for
every item of expense, including loading, discharging, port fees,
and all expenses in connection with the cargo, as well as the operat
ing charges such as crew wages, subsistence, and fuel. 10 Under the
"net" form, the owner pays all normal ship operating costs, and the
charterer is responsible for charges accrued for loading and dis
charging the cargo as well as for port fees (except those related
directly and solely to the crew) exacted against ship and cargo. The
owner retains the full burden of managing the ship.
A voyage charter, as has been pointed out, is concerned with
moving a particular cargo between designated ports. It is under
stood that the charterer will provide sufficient cargo to fill the entire
ship unless the contract stipulates otherwise. The master must
assure tnat the loading of the vessel is correct and seaworthy; almost
always he has authority to decrease or increase the quantity of cargo
offered by the shipper, as may be required to give the ship proper
trim and stability.
The charterer, for his part, must pay the agreed sum for each
ton of the commodity he has contracted to ship; ifheloads less than
the ship's capacity, he is liable for the difference between what
should have been loaded and what actually was put into the hull
("dead freight"). The owner intends for the master to accept as
much as the safety of the ship will permit, in order to obtain the
maximum revenue from the voyage. It is expected that the charter
er, having set the date when he will be ready to receive the ship, will
have the cargo waiting so that no time will be lost once the ship has
been tendered. Any delay on the charterer's part subjects him to the
possibility of paying demurrage.

## 34 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Unlike the revenue earned under the voyage charter, which is
based on the payment per ton of cargo loaded, the time charter hire
is predicated upon the carrying capacity of the ship. That is to say,
the ship has a deadweight capacity, always determined at the sum
mer load line, and so much enclosed cargo space. The charterer has
the full use of that capacity and space for the duration of the
charter. He may load any cargoes not prohibited by the charter
party, and may direct the ship to proceed wherever he desires
within the limits fixed by the contract. The charterer has no connec
tion with the navigation and operation of the vessel; he provides
cargoes for the ship, keeps her busy, and pays the owner at the
agreed rate per deadweight ton per month. If he fails to find
employment for the vessel, he still must pay the owner the stipulat
ed hire. The owner provides the crew, stores, and all necessary
equipment for the vessel; pays all port charges incurred for and in
behalf of the crew; and is responsible in all respects for the naviga
tion and maintenance of the vessel, and the stowage, care, and
safety of the cargo. The charterer provides the fuel; pays port
entry, clearance, and agency fees; pilotage charges, tug hire, costs
of loading and discharging cargo, including wharfage and dock
age; and arranges for appropriate representation in the ports dur
ing voyages. 11
To illustrate the theory of the time charter, here is a simplified
form of agreement for the use of a ship:
This charter-party is made and concluded in New York the first day
of October 1981 between Marine Carriers Company, of London,
England, owners of the good British motors hip Sea Merchant, and
Ocean Trading Corporation, of Newark, New Jersey, charterers of
the said motors hip (described as being of the highest class at Lloyd's
Register of Shipping, and of about 711,000 cubic feet grain capacity,
10,300 tons gross register, about 6,200 tons net register, and about
15,150 tons of 2,240 pounds deadweight capacity on a summer draft
of 30 feet, with a service speed of 16J;2 knots on a consumption for all
purposes of about 25 tons of best grade fuel oil), now in service and
trading. These particulars are not guaranteed, but are supplied in
good faith and are believed to be correct.
It is agreed that the vessel is tight, staunch, and strong, and in every
way fitted for the contemplated service. She is to be delivered to the
charterers in Galveston, Texas, not earlier than October 13, 1981, and
not later than October 30, 1981, and redelivered to the owners in a

## TRAMP SHIPPING 35
safe United States Atlantic port north of Cape Hatteras but not east of
New York in about fifteen months from date of delivery.
Should the vessel not be delivered by October 30, 1981, the charter
ers have the option to cancel. If the vessel cannot be delivered by the
cancelling date, the charterers are to declare, within 48 hours (Sun
days and holidays excluded) of receiving notice from the owners,
whether they will cancel or take delivery of the vessel.
The vessel is to be employed in lawful trades to carry only lawful
merchandise, excluding explosives, livestock, sulphur, and phos
phate in bulk, between good and safe ports where she can lie always
afloat, within the limits of the marine underwriters' insurance policy,
subject to the following conditions:
1. The owners shall provide and pay for all provisions, wages,
insurance of the vessel, and consular shipping and discharging fees of
the crew, as well as for all deck and engine room stores. The owners
shall maintain the hull and machinery of the vessel in a thoroughly
efficient state throughout the duration of this charter.
2. The charterers shall pay all dock, harbor, light, and tonnage dues
at the ports of delivery and redelivery. While on hire, the charterers
shall provide and pay for all fuel, port charges, pilotage (whether
compulsory or not), tug assistance, canal, dock, and other dues and
charges and taxes, and will arrange and pay for all loading and
unloading of cargo, including trimming, stowing, weighing, tallying,
and delivery of cargo.
3. The charterers shall pay for the use and hire of the vessel at the
rate of twelve dollars and twenty eight cents per deadweight ton per
month, including overtime for the crew. Payments shall be made in
United States currency not less frequently than once every thirty days,
in advance. It is agreed that a "month" shall consist of thirty days.
4. The whole reach and burden of the vessel's holds, decks, and
usual places of loading shall be at the charterers' disposal.
5. The master shall prosecute his voyages with all reasonable speed,
and all vessel logs shall be shown to the charterers on demand. The
master shall be diligent in his care of the cargo, and shall supervise the
loading, stowing, trimming, and discharge of cargo. He shall sign bills
of lading as presented, in conformity with mate's or tally clerks'
receipts.
6. Should the vessel be hindered or prevented from performing
under this charter-party for longer than thirty six consecutive hours
by reason of drydocking, or other measures necessary to maintain the
ship, or as a result of breakdowns of machinery, strikes of ship's
personnel, or damage to hull or machinery, hire shall be suspended

## 36 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
from the time the difficulty began until the vessel is returned to full
serVICe.
7. The owners shall have a lien upon all cargoes and subfreights
belonging to the charterers and any freight payable under bills of
lading, and the charterers shall have a lien upon the vessel for all
moneys paid in advance and not earned.
8. The vessel is to work day and night if required. The owners shall
pay any overtime compensation earned by officers and crew as a result
of working beyond their agreed hours.
9. Any dispute arising under this charter is to be referred to arbitra
tion in New York, N. Y., U.S.A. One arbitrator shall be nominated by
owners and one by charterers, with the third to be chosen by agree
ment of both arbitrators.
10. A commission of 2 Y2 percent is payable by the owners to the
brokers on any hire paid under this charter.
Signed for the owners Signed for the charterers
Marine Carriers Company Oceanic Trading Corporation
by Harold T. Seaman by William T. Merchant
as broker only as broker only
The bareboat charter is similar to the time charter in that the use
of the ship is acquired for a period of time, but the full responsibil
ity of operation is transferred from owner to charterer. Obviously,
a heavy burden is placed upon any person taking a ship under such
an agreement. Few deals of this sort are consummated; for the most
part they are the result of complex financing transactions in which
the legal owner is not a ship-operating organization but a bank or
similar institution. The charterer must have a complete staff in
existence to assume the duties of running the ship. Nearly always
steamship companies which charter ships on a bareboat basis do so
to augment their fleet capabilities either for a short period of peak
cargoes, or over the long term in lieu of purchasing a ship.
Perusal of the different charter-parties is rewarding, for the
numerous provisions of the documents reflect the experience of
shipowners and charterers through the years. Drawn by practical
shipping men working alongside experts in maritime and commer
cial law, the charter-parties represent a working compromise be
tween the demands of the shipowners and the insistent require
ments of the charterers. Negotiations for a particular transaction
usually start with one of these standardized, preprinted forms, with

## TRAMP SHIPPING 37
appropriate insertions of agreed terms. If necessary, the clauses are
modified to fit the circumstances of the proposed charter.
"Working the market" requires that both charterer and ship
owner be alert to many factors. The charterer needs to know a great
deal about the availability of ships and the consequent fluctuations
of the freight and charter markets. He must note how rates change
for the same commodity on different trade routes and at varying
times of the year. The operator has to be aware of practically
everything the charterer should know while keeping at his fin
gertips a wide assortment of facts and details which are part and
parcel of his business life. He must be cognizant of conditions in
ports around the world, the charges made for handling his ships,
the efficiency of longshoremen, and the availability of cargo-work
ing equipment. He has to be alert to the peculiarities of harbors
their depths, tides and winds, ice and fog anchorages, and local
customs concerning the use of pilots and tugs. He is wise also to be
informed of ship repair facilities in those ports. He needs contacts
in anticipated ports of call which will furnish current, reliable, and
prompt reports of all developments affecting shipping in those
waters. Where to procure fuel is a never-ending cause of concern,
especially as the quality of that which is offered today is subject to
wide variation.
When it comes to cargoes, the tramp shipowner must be inti
mately acquainted with the characteristics of practically every com
modity in the world that moves in bulk, so that when he sets a rate it
will be fairly certain to earn a reasonable revenue from each ton
carried. Finally, he must know how these bulk commodities are
handled, for in some ports everything is mechanized and therefore
assures fast loading or unloading, while in other ports the process is
altogether by hand.
It is small wonder that mastering the intricacies of the tramp
shipping business is a full-time career!

## CHAPTER 3 - The Management of Tramp Shipping
To
PROVIDE timely and efficient transportation
from a producing area separated from a consuming region by
thousands of miles of ocean is both a highly complex business and a
very severe test of the business judgment and skill of those responsi
ble for the effective employment of tramp ships. Not every vessel is
adapted to carry every cargo, and not every port has a constant
stream of ships entering and leaving. Some commodities are sea
sonal in their movements, while others provide a steady flow month
after month and year after year. Some items are easy to load and
discharge, and cause no structural damage, whereas others are
troublesome and dangerous. On some routes, competition between
tramps is intense, which means that the margins of profit are
narrow, if they exist at all. Other routes, for a variety of reasons,
offer more acceptable rates of compensation, but fewer opportuni
ties. Certain ports are renowned for prompt dispatch, while others
seem to be afflicted with insoluble problems of delays and the
resulting congestion. Matching ships to cargoes, selecting the most
advantageous routings, ascertaining accurately the costs of per
formance under different types of contracts, and balancing present
opportunities against future prospects, are a few of the areas of
concern in the profitable and effective management of tramp ships.
As is true in any form of commercial enterprise, the motivating
purpose must dominate the decision-making process. In tramp
shipping, the purpose is to transport cargoes at the most profitable
level of compensation. The commodities that move in greatest
quantity, and therefore offer the best opportunities for employ
ment of ships, are ores, grains, and coal. Of these, grain is the most
likely to experience fluctuations in demand, and therefore to have a
significant impact upon the tramp shipping industry. This fact
helps to explain why grain-a term which includes a wide variety of

## THE MANAGEMENT OF TRAMP SHIPPING 39
cereals such as wheat, corn, oats, barley, rye, sorghums, and soyas
has been called "the great leveler" of the shipping business. As a
basic requirement in feeding the population of the world, grain is in
constant demand. It is grown in many regions, but only the United
States, Canada, Argentina, and Australia (in that descending order
of magnitude) have significant quantites for export.! As a cargo,
grain can be carried in many different types of ships, and because it
moves almost exclusively in bulk, it is susceptible to mechanized
handling, with resultant short loading and discharging times. Until
the advent of containerships and the introduction of the very large
bulk carriers, grain was an attractive "filler" cargo for ships in the
liner service, and therefore became the target of intense competi
tion between "regular traders" and tramps.
A different aspect of competition became manifest when the
large, fast, specialized containerships were introduced to these
routes and, at about the same time, the tankers and bulkers of at
least 50,000 tons deadweight capacity began actively to seek grain
cargoes. Both these types were able to offer freight rates which,
because of economies of scale, were significantly below those which
the conventional 12,000- to 16,000-ton tramp could accept. It is
worthy of note that the movement of grain on the North Atlantic
trade route is dominated by large-capacity vessels.
The tramp ship, however, has not been eliminated from the
North Atlantic. Despite rigorous competition, the versatility ofthis
vessel, with the advantage of being able to enter ports too shallow
for the deep draft bulkers, has made possible satisfactory employ
ment in a variety of cargo movements. Grain continues to be a
commodity which these smaller ships can and do transport as they
fight a successful battle to survive in an increasingly competitive
environment. Many other dry bulk cargoes move in international
commerce, and provide year-round opportunities for bold and
aggressive owners of tramp ships. Metallic ores, fertilizers, coal, and
forest products, to name only a few, account for a substantial
tonnage which must be transported across the seas. To meet the
incessant demand for cargo space entails vigorous action on the
part of all concerned: producers of these materials, traffic manag
ers, ship brokers, and shipowners. Each of these groups needs to
have, in varying degrees, a world-encompassing store of knowledge
on which to base decisions.

## 40 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The "Fortune" class of 20,OOO-ton, 15.5-knot general-purpose carriers
was built in Japan to replace World War II Liberty ships. Photo: Ishikawa
jima Harima Heavy Industries.
It is not surprising, therefore, to discover that both the users of
tramp ships and the owners of those hard-working carriers become
avid readers of the daily news, with special emphasis upon accounts
of weather in different areas, economic conditions, political events,
and crop reports. A sudden flood in midsummer, when crops are
ripening, and only minor quantities of rainfall are needed, may
have devastating effects on the final harvest. Not only is the farmer
involved immediately, but the sellers and importers will have to
begin to make adjustments in their marketing plans. Carriers
both land and oceangoing-may discover that the tonnage requir
ing their services is reduced to a trickle. The worldwide patterns of
tramp ship operations may be altered drastically as a result of this
untimely excess of water in the growing region. The ability to relate
news events to transportation activities is a prime requisite for those
who would participate in this fascinating and ever changing activity.

## THE MANAGEMENT OF TRAMP SHIPPING 41
The "S. D. 14" was designed in England, and was the most successful
peacetime series-built ship in history. Over 200 had been delivered by
1982. Photo: Turner, courtesy of Austin & Pickersgill, Shipbuilders.
For the owner and operator of tramp-type shipping, the goal
toward which all efforts are directed is the most effective employ
ment of the units of the fleet. The ultimate criterion of success is the
profit or loss sustained from the voyages undertaken during a given
accounting period. Whether the color of the ink used by the book
keeper is black, announcing a profit, or red, admitting a loss,
depends upon the ability of the managers of the fleet to reduce
nonrevenue-producing voyages in ballast to an acceptable mini
mum, to control the number of idle days between active voyages, to
find the most suitable cargoes for the ships, and to obtain the best
freight rates and the most favorable terms. Unquestionably, the
burdens placed upon the persons who must make the final deci
sions are very great, but so are the rewards when the results show
that the decisions were sound.
To be able to arrange for a series of charter commitments which
will follow one upon another with little or no intervening time
during which the ships do not earn any revenue is the ambition of
every manager. Should the policy of the owner be to keep the fleet

## 42 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
in the market for voyage charters, where the returns are higher but
the risks of finding continuous employment are greater than in the
longer term time charter activity, it is necessary to be extremely
selective in the commitments made. For example, a high rate may
be offered to carry a cargo of grain from New Orleans to Calcutta,
but unless there is some assurance that a load can be found within a
comparatively short distance from Calcutta, the business may be a
money loser because of the high cost of sending the ship to the area
of her next employment. Ideally, as each voyage charter is ended
and the ship is redelivered to the owner, a new assignment is
waiting, and the time between revenue-producing voyages is re
duced to the absolute minimum. In practice, this happens only
occasionally; all too often, it is necessary to send the ship on a trip of
several thousand miles to pick up the next load. The profits, if any,
from the first voyage may be absorbed in the cost of repositioning.
To illustrate the various possibilities for employment of a tramp
ship, this hypothetical example is offered: A 15,OOO-ton general
purpose vessel was assigned to lift grain from New Orleans to India.
By the time she had completed discharge at the port of destination,
these were the possibilities under consideration by the owner:
1. To proceed in ballast to Australia to pick up wheat for
delivery in either the United Kingdom or the Bordeaux-Hamburg
range of the European continent; or
2. To proceed in ballast from India to Mauritius to load a full
cargo of sugar for a port on the Atlantic coast of either Canada or
the northern range of the United States; or
3. To proceed to Murmugao, India, to take on a load of ore for
Japan. From Japan, there were these potential opportunities for em
ployment: (a) to sail in ballast to the Philippines to load copra for Eu
rope; (b) to go to the Philippines for a cargo of sugar for a United
States port on the Gulf of Mexico or the Atlantic coast; or (c) to pro
ceed in ballast to British Columbia for either a cargo of grain for Eu
rope, or a full load oflumber for the Atlantic coast of the United States.
In the real world of tramp shipping, there must be assurance
that the terms and conditions demanded by the charterer are fair
and reasonable from the viewpoint of the shipowner. What at first

## THE MANAGEMENT OF TRAMP SHIPPING 43
may seem to be a very profitable rate may turn out to be a guarantee
of a heavy loss if there is no protection against excessive delays
incident to port congestion. For instance, if a cargo of grain is
offered from Churchill, a Canadian port on Hudson Bay where the
weather is notoriously uncertain and often shuts down the port
altogether for several consecutive days, the owner should insist
upon the charterer agreeing to compensate him for time lost in
such an eventuality. Otherwise, despite a very attractive rate, the
voyage might result in a deficit. This is especially true in the case of
the modern large-capacity ships which have operating expenses
amounting to many thousands of dollars each day.
As a tool with which to formulate decisions relating to the future
employment of a tramp steamer, the electronic computer is of great
value, gaining greater recognition year by year. In no way, how
ever, does it supersede the commercial wisdom which tramp ship
managers have acquired through years of experience in their pro
fession. The machine is only as good as the programs by which it is
governed, and the programs of greatest utility are those which
reflect the realities of tramp shipping. Combining electronic wiz
ardry with business experience in this computer age makes it possi
ble to obtain quickly a wide variety of almost unbelievably detailed
calculations. From these products of the machine the owner can
determine which of the various choices available to him will result in
the most efficient employment of his ship.
Despite the great amount of data which the computer can
provide, the final decision as to the type of charter to accept de
pends upon the owner's ability to evaluate the calculations and to
foresee the future demands for ships of the type he controls. If it is
