The ship always must have on board sufficient fuel to make the
prescribed voyage, regardless of how much cargo may have to be
sacrificed. Once the minimum fuel requirement has been deter
mined, the question of whether to load additional oil or more cargo
will be answered only after studying the prevailing freight rates, the
costs of fuel along the route, and the potentials for profit (or
reductions in cost) which may exist.
Ships in liner service, repeating their voyages, usually take on
the same quantities of fuel, water, and stores in the same ports on
every trip. A pattern thus is established which makes bunkering a
matter of procurement rather than of planning. For example, a
vessel operating from New York to Valparaiso via several South
American ports might find that Cristobal (Panama Canal) is the
cheapest point in which to buy oil. The bunkering pattern would
call for the ship to sail from New York with sufficient fuel, plus
reserve, for the voyage to Cristobal. At that port, oil would be taken
aboard for the voyage from Cristobal to Valparaiso and return.
The reserve requirement would be computed on the basis of the
fuel needed for the last leg of the northbound passage. At Cristo
bal, homeward bound, the ship would be supplied with enough oil,
including reserve, for the passage to New York and then back to
Cristobal, where the procedure would be repeated.
As soon as the ship has been assigned to a voyage, the bunkering
staff must determine what ports are available or desirable as possi
ble fueling points. These are some of the questions that must be
answered in the process of developing the bunkering plan:
Are supplies of fuel in this port plentiful?
Is the fuel of the desired type and quality, and is it available in
the quantity required?
What is the price of fuel? Does this price include delivery
charges if lighterage is involved?
What are the physical arrangements for bringing oil to the ship
(i.e., pipeline, barge, harbor tanker)?
Can the ship take on fuel while working cargo at her berth?
If a barge is used for delivery, will it be brought to the ship after
dark? How many tons of oil can the barge carry, and what is the
pumping rate per hour from the barge into the ship's tanks?
If fueling must be accomplished only at a special fuel pier, what
fees are involved? How much time is needed to shift to this facility,

## SCHEDULING AND BUNKERING 345
and how fast is fuel loaded aboard ship?
What regulations govern the fueling of a ship at anchor in the
harbor?
Assuming that both the schedule and the bunkering plan are
approved, and orders are issued to make them effective, the oper
ating department instructs the agents in the ports of call. Among
the points to be verified are that pilots actually perform according
to contracts, that tugs can and will be available to berth and unberth
the ships, and that longshoremen, checkers, security guards, opera
tors of dockside equipment, and any other essential working or
supervisory personnel will be on hand when the ship arrives. It also
requires assurance from the port authorities, if the steamship com
pany does not have an exclusive lease on the terminal, that ap
propriate pier or wharf space with the supporting transit shed and
upland area will be clear and ready for the vessel. For shipboard
personnel, the working schedule must make certain that officers
and unlicensed mariners needed during the port call will be on
duty, and that no delays will result from failure to have the "right
man in the right place at the right time."
As soon as possible after a fueling port has been designated, the
agent must be notified. The information to be transmitted to this
representative, at the minimum, must include the name of the ship,
the estimated time of arrival, the estimated quantity and grade of
fuel oil required, the length of time the ship is scheduled to be in the
port, and the amount of cargo, if any, which will be handled during
the port call. Two days before the scheduled arrival, the master
must send a radiogram to the agent giving the time of arrival of the
vessel, and the approximate number of barrels of oil his ship will
require. This notification permits the agent to make local arrange
ments to receive and service the vessel. A confirming message
usually is sent by the master twenty-four hours before arrival to
assure that the vessel will be accorded the treatment desired. It
comes as no surprise to anyone that the agent is waiting at the berth
when the ship is being moored.
Oil companies, particularly major organizations with many for
eign affiliates and bunkering stations, offer contracts to shipowners
on a yearlong, worldwide basis. Should there be no contract, the
operator must purchase his fuel oil at the local ("spot") price, which

## 346 THE BUS I N E S S 0 F S HIP PIN G
Top: Starting platform of a twin-screw turbine-driven steamship. Photo:
Union Castle Line. Bottom: Control station for the two diesel engines of a
modern twin-screw motorship. Photo: The Motor Ship, courtesy Royal Mail
Line.

## SCHEDULING AND BUNKERING 347
is subject to daily fluctuation as a result of changes in the cost of
crude oil, tanker rates, and the normal working of the laws of
supply and demand. The contract price, on the other hand, may be
increased only after advance notice to the purchasers. Reductions
in price always are made effective immediately since they are ad
vantageous to purchasers and serve to retain goodwill. By signing a
contract, the shipowner obtains a certain degree of stability in fuel
oil costs. This permits computation of voyage costs to be made with
a satisfactory margin of accuracy at the outset of the voyage.
Because no single oil supplier has sales outlets in every port of
the world, shipowners trading in several geographical areas usually
sign contracts with more than one provider. When this is done, the
different suppliers are responsible for certain ports or for definite
geographic regions. For instance, a liner-service company operat
ing in a number of different regions might contract with M Com
pany for ports of the United States and the east coast of South
America, N Company for West African ports, 0 Company for
ports in South and East Africa, and P Company for the Mediterra
nean. By spreading the business among the various suppliers, the
shipowner is assured that all vessels will obtain fuel of acceptable
quality at the published contract price. When supplies of oil are
adequate, ships usually are bunkered on a first come, first served
basis, without regard to whether the vessels are covered by con
tracts. In the event of shortages, however, priority would be given
to vessels under contract.
Into the bunkering schedule are written the names of each port
on the ship's itinerary, the places where she will take on fuel oil, the
estimated quantity to be loaded, and the amount of oil the ship is
expected to consume between bunkerings. If the vessel is engaged
in conventional liner service, and her refueling points remain prac
tically unchanged voyage after voyage, the responsibility of prepar
ing the bunkering schedule may be assigned to ajunior member of
the operating department, usually in the office of the superinten
dent engineer. Should the ship be assigned to tramping operations,
especially under voyage charters, the senior executives may either
prepare the bunkering plan themselves, or insist on approving it
before it becomes effective. This results from the fact that the
earning power of the ship is affected directly by the ports selected
and the quantities and prices of the oil purchased.

## 348 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
In the tanker trades, a basic fact is that the quantity of fuel taken
aboard invariably reduces the payload. The impact of this upon
bunkering practice has been explained earlier in this chapter. It
also is important to appreciate that the ship delivers her cargo to
ports where oil is not naturally available. To make the tanker as
useful as possible, therefore, the proposed bunkering plan should
not envision taking any fuel out of the destination port except in
special circumstances. If, for example, the tanker is bringing a
cargo of aviation gasoline, and maximum tonnage is needed for
airline operations, the shipowner would be justified in buying ship
fuel at this port of discharge. If the cargo were the crude oil from
which ship fuel was derived, purchase of bunkers would produce
little economic advantage.
One of the reasons for using very large crude oil carriers
(250,000 tons deadweight or larger) in preference to ships of about
70,000 tons deadweight is that the big ships deliver 28 barrels of
cargo for every barrel of fuel they burn, whereas the smaller ships
deliver only 11 barrels of cargo for each barrel consumed. The
same reasoning applies in comparing diesel engines and steam
turbines; diesels use less oil per horsepower output than any other
form of seagoing power.
In making the bunkering plan for a tanker, therefore, the ideal
is to deliver the greatest possible quantity of cargo, in order to earn
maximum revenue, while removing from the discharge port the
minimum amount of fuel oil. Achievement of this ideal may be
facilitated if an intermediate and low-priced bunkering port can be
visited without significant deviation from the shortest sea distance,
and the time spent taking on the required fuel is offset by the
increased earnings of the ship.
Making the schedule for a tanker follows the same processes
used for dry-cargo carriers, except that the tanker is self-sufficient
in her ability to discharge her cargo. Regardless of the hour of the
day or night when she arrives at the receiving terminal's facility, a
tanker commences cargo operations immediately. Ifs he is to load at
this port, the delivery of cargo begins as soon as the hoses from
shoreside tanks are connected. If the vessel is to discharge, her
pumps are started when all hoses have been matched with shore
piping systems. No large crews oflongshoremen or cargo checkers
are needed; usually permanently assigned terminal personnel han-

## SCHEDULING AND BUNKERING 349
dIe all details. Relatively few tankers are sent to more than one port
to discharge, although multiple port unloading is not an isolated
phenomenon. Hardly ever does a tanker deliver a cargo and then
take on an outgoing load, simplifying the problem of port turn
around time to that extent.
The dry-cargo tramp trade is governed in its bunkering and
scheduling practices by the same principles that apply to the liner
and tanker operations. There is still the need to load cargo as
quickly as possible. In the negotiations for a voyage charter, the
number of days allotted for loading and discharge is fixed, with
corresponding bonus and penalty for reducing or exceeding these
limits. The master must supervise operations and keep an accurate
record of time used and activities pursued. Bunkering for a trip
under a voyage charter entails the same considerations as in the
liner trade: if the cargo is bulky, then the port where the ap
propriate fuel can be obtained at least cost is chosen, while for dense
cargoes the revenue from each ton of cargo carried is compared
with the price of fuel oil to determine where and in what quantity
the fuel is to be acquired. To illustrate the process, the imaginary
voyage of the Lucky Lady will be followed.
The Lucky Lady, a diesel-powered vessel of 18,150 tons dead
weight, has been chartered for a voyage from Hampton Roads to
San Antonio, Chile, carrying coal at a rate which barely meets
operational expenses. The terms of the charter permit the owner to
load not less than 15,000 tons and not more than 17,000 tons of
coal. Northbound, the Lucky Lady has been fixed for a part load of
6,000 tons of slab copper from San Antonio to Baltimore, and all
the bagged sugar the ship can accommodate from Callao, Peru, to
New York City. The freight rate on the copper is at the break-even
level, but the rate on sugar is very profitable.
The essential facts influencing the decisions required for the
bunkering plan were: (1) the ship consumed 21 tons of fuel per day
at sea, and 7 tons per day when working cargo in port; (2) there was
no diesel fuel available in San Antonio; (3) fuel could be purchased
in Callao, but at a high price; and (4) the lowest priced fuel was sold
in Panama.
To reduce the cost of the voyage, it was determined to buy only
enough fuel in Hampton Roads to reach Panama. There, fuel for
the voyage from Panama to San Antonio and return would be

## 350 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Computer-oriented engine controls are centralized in a soundproof room
aboard a modern containership. Photo: Seatrain Lines.
purchased. When the Lucky Lady came through Panama enroute to
Baltimore and New York, she would be supplied with oil for that
passage.
Steaming time from Hampton Roads to Panama was 4.75 days;
from Panama to San Antonio, 7 days; from San Antonio to Callao,
3.5 days; from Callao to Panama, 3.5 days. The passage from
Panama to Baltimore would require 5.2 days, and from that city to
New York approximately a half-day. Total fuel consumption at sea
was calculated to be 565 tons; the ship's tanks had a capacity of 800
tons.
The quantity of revenue cargo was established by the tonnage of
fuel, stores, and water that had to be aboard when the ship de
parted Panama southbound, and from Callao northbound with the
combined loads of copper slabs and bagged sugar. Fuel needs were
415 tons for steaming, 56 tons for reserve fuel, and 19 tons for
cargo work, for a total of 490 tons; water and stores added another
350 tons to the ship requirements, and left 17,310 tons for cargo.
Pursuant to terms ofthe charter, 17,000 tons of coal were loaded in
Hampton Roads. Northbound from Callao, the ship had to allow
109 tons for the trip to Baltimore, plus 28 tons reserve; four days of

## SCHEDULING AND BUNKERING 351
cargo work required 28 tons of oil, and the short distance from
Baltimore to N ew York needed only 11 tons. Stores and water
remained at the constant figure of 350 tons, so the maximum
tonnage of sugar that could be loaded came to 11,624 tons.
The bunkering plan and schedule were developed with all
aspects of the ship's employment in mind. Full advantage was taken
of the flexibility granted by the charters for both the coal and sugar
cargoes, and fuel was purchased at the least expensive point. By
these devices, the greatest revenue was earned from the ship's
employment.
In summary, it may be stated that the successful activity of any
ship depends upon the skill with which the schedule is developed,
the manner in which advantage is taken of the characteristics and
capabilities of the ship, and the nature of the cargo as well as the
prevailing freight rates. Customer desires for the shortest possible
transit times and the requirement by the owner that the ship be
used effectively and efficiently usually may be combined to satisfy
all parties concerned. In the tanker and many dry-cargo tramping
trades, deadweight cargo is carried almost as a matter of routine.
Because only cargo produces revenue, the accurate determination
of how to fuel the ship is vital to owner and operator. That the
intricacies and possible pitfalls of planning the scheduling and
fueling of the ship have been mastered is one of the signs of
professionalism in ship management.

## CHAPTER 18 - PlanningforaNew Ship
I
TIS almost an axiom of the steamship business that
the best ship for any trade is one that has been designed expressly to
meet the particular needs of the route to which the vessel is as
signed. This belief is not vitiated by the fact that many ships have
been built in assembly line fashion to a single pattern, and have
been used in widely different services. Standardized ships are ac
cepted only because the cost of planning, designing, and building
ships intended particularly for certain trades is so great. Every
shipowner cherishes a dream of the perfect ship for one special
type of employment, and every ship built to individual specifica
tions embodies as much of those dreams as the vision and skill of the
naval architect can transform into reality.
Dreams shatter on contact with reality, and dreams of perfect
ships often vanish when faced with the harsh facts of economics.
Dreams must yield to the newest developments in marine engineer
ing and naval architecture as well as in packaging and cargo-han
dling techniques. The dreamer also must accept the unavoidable
mandate that the ship earn her way and pay a profit. It becomes the
part of owner, naval architect, marine engineer, traffic manager,
and operations chief to combine their capabilities in the endeavor
to make the ship an asset throughout her economic life.
Converting dreams into the tangible reality of a ship follows an
orderly and systematic process that begins with examination of the
trade route to be served. This involves study of the cargoes that are,
and may be expected to be, carried, the extent of existing and
anticipated competition, and the efficiency of the ships now on the
route. The purpose of this examination is to describe with nicety
the mission of the new ship. Competition must be evaluated, for on
the capability of the new ship to offer better transportation than
any other vessel depends the economic success of the venture. Of

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 353
Lykes Lines' barge carrier ("Seabee") can transport inland waterways
barges as well as containers. Photo: Lykes Lines.
almost equal importance is the determination of what cargoes will
be carried, and the methods of packaging which are now in use and
may be adopted in the next ten or twenty years. The wrong deci
sion, as for instance the selection of a cellular containership in
preference to a more versatile combination type, may doom the
vessel even before she sails on her first voyage. To make the correct
choice is especially critical when it is recalled that a break-bulk cargo

## 354 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
carrier costs at least twenty million dollars, and a containership
cannot be obtained for less than fifty million dollars.
The following list demonstrates the areas of concern and the
depths of inquiry necessary to assure that the ship will fulfill her
mISSIOn:
Basic Data
1. Anticipated years of operational life of the ship.
2. Ports of call, length in miles and duration in days of each voyage,
and number of voyages planned for each year.
3. Estimated average tonnage of cargo to be loaded on outbound
and homeward passages of each voyage. Types and quantity
and the maximum weights of extra-heavy-lift items must be
indicated separately.
Traffic
4. Total deadweight tonnage and cubic capacity required in the
ship. If refrigerated cargo is to be carried, the tons of this kind
of cargo, and the number of cubic feet of space needed, must be
determined separately.
5. Liquid cargo capacity (i.e., number and characteristics of deep
tanks and other liquid cargo spaces) essential in this trade.
6. Cubic capacity for dry bulk cargo and the various types of this
cargo to be carried (number and special features of below-decks
spaces allocated to dry bulk cargo, or usable for this kind of
cargo).
7. Cubic capacity and deadweight tonnage of special cargo lockers.
If special features are required, they must be identified.
8. Provision for stowage of containers (size of boxes, number,
weight, stowage plan, number of containers to be carried under
decks and number to be stacked on deck).
9. Provisions for handling wheeled vehicles (square footage, by
decks; access arrangements; stowage and securing of vehicles).
10. Provisions for handling heavy-lift cargoes.
Operations
11. Location of bunkering ports on the trade route(s) to which the
ship normally will be assigned.
12. Propulsion equipment, consumption per day, and type of fuel.

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 355
13. Harbor and port restrictions (depth of channel at low tide in the
port with the least depth of water; berthing arrangements;
depth of water at the berth; canal locks, including dimensions of
lock chambers; dock entrance widths, and depth of water on the
sills; tide ranges).
Port Cargo Handling Facilities
14. Equipment in ports of call (availability, number, type, and loca
tion within the port of fixed dockside equipment to unload and
load bulk cargoes; container terminals; floating or land-based
heavy-lift cranes).
15. Supporting facilities (provision for storage of dry and liquid
bulk cargoes; railroad and highway vehicles' access to ship's
side).
16. Height of booms of container-handling cranes which project
over the ship's berthing space.
Regulatory Agency Requirernents
17. Government regulations affecting ship construction.
18. Classification society requirements.
19. Load line rules.
20. Requirements relating to subdivisions of the hull.
21. Limitations affecting hull admeasurements.1
Once the mission has been determined, the owner can translate
it into proposals on which the naval architect can base suggestions
for a preliminary design, and can commence a series of technical
studies leading to specifications of length, beam, depth of hull,
draft, speed of the ship and resultant shape of the underwater
body, and horsepower. As these ideas take on more substance, it
becomes feasible to prepare outlines on which to base an initial
estimate of construction costs. When this is available, the financial
and traffic managers evaluate the revenue-producing capability of
the ship in contrast to the cost.
To ascertain the maximum price which the prospective owner
can afford, it is satisfactory to make pro forma calculations which
show what the vessel mlist earn per ton of cargo carried if she is to
pay operating expenses, construction cost, and interest. For exam
ple, it is estimated that a tanker of 30,000 tons deadweight will cost

## 358 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
from builders who competed for the business by submitting bids;
the lowest was deemed the most satisfactory. The idea had yet to be
appreciated of combining purchase price and full-life operating
cost to determine whether a certain kind of ship was economically
desirable.
About 1960, several developments occurred which had the
effect of revolutionizing both the business of hauling goods across
the seas and the techniques of management of ships and shipyards.
The first and probably most important was the introduction of the
electronic data processing machine, now known worldwide as the
computer. This calculator suddenly made it possible to obtain an
assortment of data which permitted analysis in depth of many
different proposals, suggestions, and alternative ideas. The com
puter transformed ship design into a many-sided scientific opera
tion involving economic studies, financial analyses, and systematic
appraisals of not one but many possible types of vessels for a single
route.
It was probably only a coincidence of history, but at about the
same time that computer technology became an accepted tool of
management, there was an upsurge in the demand for raw mate
rials of all sorts. From 1919 to approximately 1960, the dry bulk
cargoes of world commerce moved predominantly in ships of not
more than 15,000 tons deadweight capacity. In the petroleum
trades, the switch to tankers of 25,000 tons began shortly after
World War II, and by 1960 these transporters had reached, and in
some cases exceeded, 50,000 tons deadweight. The beginning of
the decade of the 1960s marked the start of the sudden growth in
dry-cargo ship capacities. In the bulk trades, especially to transport
ores, the trend was toward ships of 40,000 to 50,000 tons dead
weight. Liner-service ships were converted to handle containers,
and in numerous instances they were enlarged. At the end of the
decade, tankers of over 300,000 tons deadweight no longer were
news; "bulkers" able to lift from 100,000 to 150,000 tons were
numerous; break-bulk ships in liner service were almost uniformly
of 15,000 tons deadweight, and containerships varied from 20,000
to 40,000 tons. For most of these big ships, an increase in speed
accompanied the growth in size.6
Throughout this time, the capability of the computer to per
form an extraordinary variety of analytical calculations was being

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 359
applied to both the shipowning and the shipbuilding industries.
There was abundant reason to capitalize upon every advantage
offered by the computer as the cost of new construction soared to
unprecedented heights and the expenses of operation increased
year by year. To forecast with greatest accuracy the potential reve
nue which a ship could earn was essential to financial stability in the
steamship business. Many owners turned to the computer. A new
atmosphere began to prevail as computer experts were called upon
to assist management personnel, and novel techniques, start
ing with analyses of projected shipbuilding programs, were
introduced.
To understand the role of the computer and the specialists
responsible for their activity, it is necessary to look briefly at the
steamship business as it exists today.
In the dry-cargo trades, ships are employed either in liner
service or in tramping. Liner service implies repeated voyages on
the same sea route, with calls at specified ports. If the owner is well
established, with long experience in this particular operation, and
plans to build a ship to replace an obsolescent unit in the fleet, it is
relatively simple to distinguish those features of the trade which
influence the design of the ship. If, however, a completely new
route is to be served, the owner must conduct whatever research is
required to determine why ships now operated on that route are
designed as they are, and how those ships accomplish their mission.
If it appears that the existing ships are deficient in one or more
respects, the prospective owner must determine how to improve
the design of the proposed vessels to assure that they will meet the
requirements of the trade efficiently and economically.
A cargo liner in all probability will transport a rather well
defined variety of items in predictable quantities arid packages.
Study of the specific design of the ship therefore may be con
centrated upon arrangements for cargo stowage and the means of
handling that cargo. If booms or cranes are to be installed, their
capacity must be examined in the light of the shipper's existing and
anticipated practices. To minimize the time and cost ofloading and
unloading ships, the sizes of hatches have been increased greatly
over the years, reaching what may be almost the structural limits in
the Delta Argentina class of ships built in 1968. These vessels had
four rows of hatches, each row consisting of three parallel open-

## 360 THE B USIN ESS OF SH IPPI NG
ings, plus one row of three openings. The effect was to give direct
access to almost every part of the stowage area below decks. As an
added feature, the ships of this class had high-speed cranes to serve
the hatches, plus a 75-ton Stuelcken boom erected between two sets
of hatches.
Equally important in the consideration of cargo handling and
stowage capability is the amount of usable space within the hull.
Ideally, cargo holds and 'tween decks should be as nearly rectangu
lar as possible and completely free of obstructions. Very fast ships
usually have fine lines which create virtually unusable areas in the
forward and after holds. Not only is cubic footage lost, but dunnage
must be used to fill the voids into which cargo cannot be placed.
Where the design of the ship produces such areas, they should be
converted to cargo deep tanks or fuel oil capacity, or to tonnage
exempted ballast spaces.
Should the owner contemplate a ship for worldwide tramping,
several decisions must be made before the essential analyses can be
initiated. Is the ship to be put into a specialized trade, such as the
carriage of either iron ore or grain? If so, the dominant characteris
tics of the commodities to be handled and the various loading and
discharge ports-the potential employment, in other words-must
be fixed in order that the vessel may have operational flexibility. If
the ship is to be a general trader, she would be equipped with the
minimum outfit of cargo booms or cranes to enable her to work in
any port. This illustrates the principle that the shallowest and least
well equipped port of call has a disproportionate impact upon
design.
In contrast to the tramp owner, the liner-service operator is
committed for the long term to a particular route. As ports are
developed and variations are experienced in the flow of goods, the
ships can be adapted to these changes. When new construction is
appropriate, the experience of the recent past has a significant
influence upon ship design. For example, the rapid conversion of
major trade routes from break-bulk to container carriage resulted
in the withdrawal of a number of comparatively new, well
equipped, and expensive break-bulk vessels and the substitution of
purpose-built containerships. The installation of shoreside contain
er-hoisting cranes in the ports between which these ships operated
made it feasible to omit hoisting gear from the ships but limited
them to sailing only to those highly developed ports.

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 361
The 160-ton capacity centerline cargo ramp of the Hellenic Innovator can
be positioned to port or starboard to work cargo. Photo: Jeff Blinn,
courtesy Moran Towing Co.
Sea-Land Service commenl:ed transoceanic operations with ful
ly equipped ("self-sustaining") ships. It soon proved to be more
efficient and profitable to construct dockside cranes and to design
ships with no cargo-handling capability. So long as the ships plied
only between equipped ports, there were no handicaps to trade. In
1980, however, Sea-Land opened a new service to Middle Eastern
ports which had no container-handling cranes at dockside. To meet
the demands of the shippers for container transportation, the
company equipped four ships with the requisite gantry cranes,
having calculated that the cost of procuring, installing, and main
taining the shipboard cranes, as well as the loss in cargo deadweight
incurred by the presence of the heavy cranes, would be more than
offset by the greater freight earnings.

## 362 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The versatility of a roll-on/roll-off ship was proven by this walk-on/walk
off cargo. Photo: Atlantic Container Line.
If a roll-on/roll-off ship is envisioned, present practice is to
include a massive ramp at the stern which can be lowered to the
apron of the pier or wharf to which the ship is moored. The
flexibility thus conferred upon the ship's employment is a distinct
advantage in establishing and maintaining service to those ports to
which are destined extremely heavy trailer-mounted items and
large numbers of wheeled vehicles.
Recent developments in the more heavily patronized routes
have produced ships which combine the features of the container
ship and the roll-on/roll-off carrier. A rather well publicized photo
graph of a file of elephants walking ashore from such a combination
ship dramatized the flexibility and adaptability of this type of de
sign. Cargo which otherwise would be shipped on break-bulk ves
sels because it could not be containerized would be loaded on
trailers to eliminate handling at the loading and unloading termi
nals. Heavy, usually over-size, pipe exemplifies this cargo. The

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 363
Delta Lines' Del Oro had many hatch openings to improve cargo-working
characteristics. Her service speed was 18.6 knots, and her deadweight was
13,500 tons. Photo: Delta Lines.
shipper secures it to a trailer, often built expressly for the purpose,
and the consignment is handled as a single unit from reception by
the carrier until delivery to the consignee at destination.
For ships engaged in the break-bulk trade, a full set of cargo
booms and their supporting equipment must be provided if one of
the ports to be visited with some regularity does not operate dock
side cranes. Inasmuch as heavy-lift cranes, either fixed or floating,
are available in most ports, owners may elect not to install the
expensive and cumbersome 'jumbo" boom in their ships.
In considering how to adapt the ship to carry cargo, it is essential
to take into account the nature and quantity of whatever is offered
for transportation. In the break-bulk liner trade, there is so much
variety in the normal load that emphasis is placed primarily on the
ease and efficiency with which cargo can be worked. As was pointed
out in a treatise on naval architecture:
It is well to agree on the fact that what constitutes an efficient layout
from the point of view of cargo carrying and cargo handling is greatly
dependent upon the special requirements of each trade. It is obvious

## 364 THE BUSINESS OF SH I PPI NG
The 22-knot "lighter aboard ship" (LASH) Benjamin Harrison was built in
1980 to carry 80 lighters and I 19 containers. Photo: McAllister Bros.
that if a great preponderance of the cargo moving in a given trade
route consists of long rails, piling, pipes, etc., the optimum ship for
that trade should have extra long and deep holds, long hatches,
centerline pillars, special rig to facilitate handling extra long drafts of
cargo in and out of the holds, etc. Extra long holds result in very large
bale cubic per cargo gear; extra long hatches result in a smaller
number of hatches, and a smaller amount of cargo handling gear, per
ship. A ship designed with the object of being optimum for handling
and stowing long pieces of cargo would not be an optimum ship to
carry general cargoes. The ability of the ship to survive damage
involving flooding also may be lessened.
An operator who wants to move general cargoes in and out of a
large number of ports, on the other hand, has no special interest in
extra long holds and hatches. He will prefer to have as many hatches,
and cargo handling gears, as he can get satisfactorily in the ship, in
order to facilitate reaching and moving the cargo that he must handle
in each port, with a minimum of interference. The requirements of
the majority of long trade routes will average somewhere between
these extremes.7
In those trades where the volume of cargo offered justifies the
installation, refrigeration or specially ventilated spaces may be in
cluded in the design of the ship. Vessels operated in the dry bulk

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 365
Two loaded barges each weighing 1,000 tons are hoisted by an electric
elevator at the stern of the Almeria Lykes. Photo: Lykes Lines.
trades usually depend upon shore facilities for both loading and
discharge; on the Great Lakes, however, many ships built after
1975 have been self-unloaders. To obviate at least to some extent
the unprofitable ballast voyages that characterize both dry bulk
carriers and tankers, the combination ore-bulk-oil ship was de
veloped. On separate voyages, these vessels can carry full cargoes of
metallic ores or grain or oil. Although a number of these ships has
been built, many have not been able to exploit their versatility on a
consistent basis.
Speed is important to all parties concerned with ocean transpor
tation. To the shipper, it reduces the time before payment for his
goods can be expected. To the consignee, it is the basis for planning
the level of inventory that must be maintained until the next ship
ment arrives.To the shipowner, it fixes the number of ships needed
to maintain a certain frequency of sailings. Over the years, the
consistent demand by users of steamships has been for higher

## 366 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
speeds. The ultimate answer was made in 1972 when Sea-Land
Service introduced its big 33-knot containerships. On all the sea
routes to which these big carriers were assigned, new records were
established for cargo ships' passages, and in many instances the
records held by the fastest passenger vessels were eclipsed. Follow
ing the oil crisis of 1973, which made the cost of fuel the major
operating expense, the cruising speed of these ships was reduced
drastically as an economy measure. In 1981-1982, the eight ships of
this class were withdrawn from commercial service and sold to the
United States Navy for conversion to amphibious operations sup
port vessels.
The prospective owner of a new ship must determine what kind
of power plant is most effective, economical, dependable, and
serviceable on the route to which the ship will be assigned, and the
type of trade in which the ship will be engaged. Diesel motors have
significant advantages up to about 50,000 brake horsepower. Steam
turbines are most efficient in the higher power ranges. Nuclear
power has been used successfully aboard naval vessels but has not
been found economical for commercial ships. Fuel has become the
most costly item of operating expense, and the choice of propulsion
equipment is critically important in the total economic life of the
vessel.
Competition has been called the soul of trade. It is undeniable
that competition does bring about change and improvement in
service. Evaluating not only existing competition but also the type
and level thereof to be faced during the anticipated lifetime of the
new ship is an essential procedure in planning. If the judgments are
correct, the investment in the ship will prove profitable; if the
forecasts are faulty, the penalty may be bankruptcy.
Although it is a basic principle of transportation economics that
the larger the vessel, the lower the unit cost of carriage, the max
imum size of ship for a particular route must be predicated upon
the quantity of cargo actually moving from port to port. The
intensity of competition, which might limit the size of a shipload,
must be taken into account in determining the revenue-producing
potential of the ship.
If, for example, there is a sustained offering of 25,000 tons of
manufactured steel per week from a single United States Atlantic
port to the Havre-Hamburg range, and four steamship companies

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 367
normally compete for that business, the maximum load to which
anyone of them reasonably should aspire would approximate ten
thousand tons. Although it is possible, in theory, for one very large
vessel to lift the entire week's production, the reality of ship opera
tion is different. Some of the facts which assail the theory are:
1. The cost of the ship's time which would be dedicated to
loading and unloading that quantity of goods, compared to the
freight revenue earned;
2. The questionable wisdom of relying upon this type of move
ment for so large a portion of vessel earnings;
3. The demands of shippers and consignees for the shortest
possible time that their goods must be in transit. "In transit time"
includes every minute from the moment of delivery to the carrier
until the consignee takes charge of the goods at destination. The
average shipper or receiver of oceanborne cargo has little interest
in the performance of the vessel, so long as his goods are delivered
quickly and in good condition.
If speed is crucial to successful competition, the cost of provid
ing record-breaking speed must be determined to the nearest dol
lar. Speed is not just adding horsepower to the engines; it entails
many refinements in hull design which affect significantly (and
often adversely) the cargo stowage capabilities of a ship. The plod
ding Liberty ship of World War II had one enormous advantage:
her hull was almost rectangular, which made possible efficient use
of all the space in the holds. By way of contrast, a postwar cargo
liner with twice the Liberty's speed had such fine hull lines that the
forward end of the hatchway in No.1 hold was almost useless for
cargo stowage. The full effect of increased speed must be deter
mined before any decision is reached; failure to consider the less
obvious aspects can be economically disastrous.
Finally, in the preliminary planning for the new ship, the com
puter permits analysis of the economic forecasts for the entire life
of the ship. A number of contingencies can be postulated, and the
computer will provide the answers.
For instance, if the ship is to be used in the tramp trade, the
projected cost of building can be made the basis for determining
the minimum freight rate or charter hire required to defray all

## 368 THE B USJ NESS OF SH J PPJ NG
The SOO-ton capacity traveling gantry crane of a lighter-carrying ship
(LASH) is prepared to hoist a lighter aboard the Bilderdyk. Photo: LASH
Systems, Inc.
operating costs and repay the investment. As part of the calcula
tion, the best of circumstances can be assumed: each year the ship
will make six voyages, will require only two weeks of shipyard time,
will be forced to travel in ballast a total of twenty days, and will

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 369
spend a maximum of ten days idle in port between charters. A
"worst case" assumption also can be made: the ship would make six
voyages, but would lose four weeks for shipyard repairs; would
steam in ballast a total of forty-five days; and be idle in port for
another thirty. Many possibilities can be programmed into the
computer to obtain a realistic picture of the ship's profitability over
the full term of her working life.
It is evident, from these examples, that the computer makes it
possible for the shipowner, the naval architect, and the shipbuilder
to perform elaborate and deeply penetrating economic studies of
the earning power of the proposed ship. Before the advent of this
electronic marvel, the calculations were either so laborious and so
slow that their use was discouraged, or the data available were so
scanty that many decisions had to be based on empirical knowledge,
experience, and (for lack of a more accurate description) intuition.
It is correct to deduce that the process of planning for a new
ship involves concurrent action in several areas of concern. At no
time may any participant in the decision-making process overlook
the fact that the ship has to earn all expenses and pay at least a
modest profit. At the same time, the new vessel must possess certain
physical characteristics that assure efficient and effective operation
in her assigned trade.
Up to this point, major attention has been devoted to economic
matters, because until these have been explored, the idea of a new
ship cannot become a reality. The naval architect should share in
many ofthese economic studies ifhis preliminary specifications and
sketches are to be included in the solution. Not until the economic
studies are finished can some basic decisions be made, of which the
size, speed, and characteristics necessary to achieve the ship's mis
sion are fundamental.
These decisions become binding once the intent to build is
approved. Suggestions for development of the design within the
approved limitations are studied and sketched by the architect so
that the management team can appraise their worth. It often trans
pires that examination of a drawing prompts improvement and
justifies reworking earlier proposals in order to accommodate the
new ideas. While these design programs are in progress, a concur
rent series of technical analyses of the evolving plan, often per
formed by carefully developed computer techniques, must be un
dertaken to assure that these requirements are satisfied:

## 370 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Centerline cranes and three hatches above each hold of the Del Oro permit
rapid and economical handling of cargo. Photo: Delta Lines.
I. Trading pattern. Does the design meet the needs of the es
tablished pattern of operation, including the ability of the ship to
serve all the ports on the route, to carry the quantity and variety of
cargo offered (or anticipated for the future), and to provide rapid
ity of transit satisfactory to the shippers?
II. Speed of the ship. Does the ship have sufficient speed at least to
equal or surpass the competition on the route? Is the proposed
speed economically sustainable given existing freight rates and
predicted prices for fuel?

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 371
III. Dimensions of the ship. Is the proposed size correct for the
ports the ship is to enter and the type and quantity of cargo she is to
carry?
IV. Hullform and displacement. Does the design incorporate the
best hydrodynamic qualities possible within the limitations imposed
by the requirements of the intended employment of the ship? Do
the hull lines produce cargo stowage spaces that are acceptable and
economical to work?
V. General arrangement of the ship. Does the design result in
maximum return from the interior layout of the ship, especially
with reference to cargo loading and discharge, but not discounting
in any way the need to stow cargo efficiently and safely? Are cargo
handling devices correctly assigned to satisfy the requirements of
the ship's employment?
VI. Cubic measurements within the hull. Are the capacities of the
cargo holds distributed appropriately, with due appreciation of the
need to balance working times for each compartment? Is cargo
handling facilitated by the internal arrangements of space?
VII. Choice ofm achinery. Has the most effective and reliable mode
of propulsion been selected, in view of the projected employment
of the ship? Is the recommended mode the most economical, in
terms of sustained performance in circumstances of maximum
strain as well as in ideal situations? If a diesel motor is being
considered, does the study evaluate the problems which can arise
from using fuel that barely meets minimum specifications?
VIII. Outfit of the ship. Are the weight and cost of different types
of cargo gear and supporting equipment proportionate to the
capabilities of the ship? What is the difference in cost between the
ideal installation and one that meets minimum specifications? What
effects can be predicted from the selection of less than ideal
outfitting?
IX. Cargo capacity and ship's total deadweight tonnage. What is the
net revenue-producing capability of the ship after fuel, water, and
stores have been placed aboard?
The answers to these questions can be found only by careful
examination of every proposal, not only for its immediate applica
bilityand practicality but also for the "second order" effects of these
ideas. If a containership is to be built, the desirability of installing

## 372 THE B USI NESS OF SH I PPING
gantry cranes to make the vessel independent of port facilities must
be analyzed to establish "first order" effects like increased costs of
building, reduced cargo deadweight tonnage, and more expensive
ship maintenance. At the same time, second order effects like
greater flexibility in scheduling ports of call, elimination of crane
hire expense, and the opportunity to work cargo at an anchorage,
must be taken into account. Only by systematic evaluation of all
effects can the correct decisions be reached. It follows that once a
decision has been made for a component part of the ship, that
decision must apply to all future developments in the design. In
asmuch as many of these early stage decisions involve computations
and mutations brought about by a variety of contingencies, the
importance of the computer in the analyses and forecasts is difficult
to exaggerate.
In some instances, the analysis takes the form of elaborate
comparisons of two possibilities. A rather obvious subject for this
type of computer-assisted scrutiny is the selection of the propulsion
system for the ship. Within specific limits of horsepower, the usual
alternative to the geared turbine is a diesel motor. There are ad
vantages to the turbine, not least of which is its sturdiness and
dependability under adverse operating conditions, and the rather
large number of ship repair activities around the world which can
provide assistance if required. Compared to the diesel engine,
however, the turbine burns more oil per horsepower per hour, and
second order impact is noted in the consequential reduction in the
cargo deadweight of the ship. Diesel fuel costs more than the oil
burned under boilers to produce steam, but the diesel's lower rate
of consumption per horsepower per hour usually offsets the price
differential. If the cheapest and lowest grade of oil is used as a
matter of routine, there may be a noticeable impact upon the
operating efficiency of the motor. Primary effects which must be
studied therefore include the mechanical modifications to permit
burning low-grade oil and the anticipated higher maintenance
expenses. Second order considerations involve loss of ship time,
and hence reduced earnings, consequent upon the need for repair
work; erratic schedules, with inevitable loss of shippers' and char
terers' confidence; and lowered efficiency in overall ship perform
ance caused by undue cylinder wear from using inferior fuel. By
instructing the computer to take into account all these contin
gencies as essential elements of the analysis of the two types of

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 373
Energy Independence was the first seagoing coal-burning steamship built in
the United States since 1921. She loaded her first cargo, 32,366 long tons
of coal, at Hampton Roads on August 8, 1983. Photo: General Dynamics.
propulsion equipment, a valid basis for decision can be established.
In the wake of the oil fuel crisis of recent years, there has been a
resurgence of interest in the use of coal to generate steam. Mechan
ical stoking, improved draft, and innovative methods of burning
the coal have reduced the problems of operating the furnaces, but
significant second effects remain to be studied. There is, for in
stance, the availability of coal in ports of call and suitable equipment
to deliver it to the ship's bunker spaces. Additionally, somewhat
larger hulls may be required for the same amount of enclosed space
for cargo because of the bulkiness of the coal and the requirement
that it be adjacent to the fireroom. A third factor is the time needed
to take the coal aboard and the interruptions, if any, in working
cargo caused by this fueling procedure. That solutions exist was
borne out by the construction (or conversion) in 1982 and 1983
of eight coal-burning ships. These vessels were produced in the
United States, Italy, Japan, Korea, and Spain, and ranged in size

## 374 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
from 33,000 tons deadweight to 151,500 tons deadweight. They
have been operated successfully by Australian, United States, and
Spanish owners.s
In preparing the analysis of full-life economy of the proposed
vessel, a number of assumptions must be made and these become
major elements in programming the computer. It is an absolute
requirement of this analysis that the assumptions be as realistic as
possible, based upon the recent experience of the operator and the
latest available information, both statistical and otherwise. The
amount of cargo that a ship actually lifts for a voyage is a critical
matter; to assume that she sails completely full on every trip when
in fact the average load occupies not more than 80 percent to 85
percent of the available space is to create a totally false picture of the
earning power of the ship. Seasonal variations in cargo tonnage also
must be taken into account, especially if the new ship is to be larger
than the one to be replaced. Another assumption which bears on
the full-life capability of the vessel is speed. Some operators insist
upon the same speed being maintained on every voyage and
achieve this result by increasing the horsepower output as the hull
accumulates barnacles and marine growth. Other operators stipu
late that the engines may produce a certain maximum horsepower,
and hold to that output regardless of the effect on ship speed. If the
sea route passes through areas which are notorious for adverse
weather conditions at certain times of the year, and voyages take
longer because of the need to safeguard the ship, this fact also must
be included. Finally, as equipment ages, it often is less capable of
optimum performance than when it was new. To instruct the
computer, therefore, that all voyages for the life of the ship are to
be calculated at a standard speed is to invite major errors.
The economic study of ship design is an extremely complex
matter, especially as computers explore many avenues previously
inaccessible to management. Many mistakes can be avoided by
subjecting both the preliminary intentions to build a new ship and
the proposals for actual construction to a methodical and systemati
cally developed program of analysis. Excessive reliance on the
"wisdom" of the computer, however, is contrary to the best interests
of the prospective operator. The experience of veteran members of
the staff, the accrued knowledge of business trends which has been
built up by actual participation in ocean transportation, the per
formance history of existing units of the fleet, and records of

## PLANNING FOR A NEW SHIP 375
achievement are as much in demand today as they ever were. The
computer's results will be only as good as the instructions given to
the machine, and those instructions necessarily are derived from
the combined contributions of all the persons involved.
The introduction of computer technology to steamship man
agement coincided with some of the major changes in transoceanic
transportation, of which containerization was but one facet. Ship
capacities and cruising speeds were increased, and a wide variety of
improved cargo handling systems became available. Simultaneous
ly, shipbuilding costs escalated noticeably. The old procedure of
improving existing models of ships, without any radical departures
from established practice, no longer was satisfactory. By linking the
computer and ship managers' need for a better basis for decisions,
it became possible to project ten or twenty years ahead to establish
the profit-making potential of a ship under a wide range of contin
gencies. That previously elusive figure, the freight rate (or charter
hire) required to break even, was given both substance and credibil
ity when realistic assumptions and actual experience could be sup
plied to the computer.
Steamship owners, despite the incursion of the "thinking ma
chine," still must make decisions affecting the future welfare of
their organizations. They continue to consult with the naval ar
chitect and the marine engineer in seeking the proper interpreta
tion of data supplied by the computer. Aided by economic analyses,
and especially by full-life forecasts of costs and revenues, the actions
taken by owners and senior managers can be based on a depth and
range of information never before available. Although it is possible
for owners to become so engrossed in the financial aspects of
management that they consider the ships they direct on their
courses only as cogs in the complex mechanism called international
trade, it remains true that it is the ships that must do battle with the
elemental forces of nature, or lie in remote and perchance exotic
harbors while being loaded or discharged, and do the bidding of
the skillful and dedicated people who make up their complements.
Computers may aid in the improvement of ship design, but every
one concerned must be aware at all times that the ship is the only
vehicle of transportation in which a number of human beings live,
work, and play for protracted periods of time as they exercise final
control over the actual performance of that admirable creation.

## CHAPTER 19 - Passenger Cruises
VACATION CRUISES-defined as the transpor
tation of pleasure-seeking travelers on ocean voyages offering one
or more glamourous ports of call-are the only elements that have
survived from the once flourishing passenger steamship business.
The importance of this type of service may be gauged by the fact
that the port of Miami, Florida, embarked approximately two mil
lion travelers during 1983. Twenty-one cruise ships made that
southern city their home port. Nationwide, the cruise business
generated revenue of five billion dollars.!
Although not a post-World War II innovation, for the history of
passenger cruises reaches back at least to 1844, the cruise business
as it exists today began in 1946. Its growth since then has been
phenomenal. Scores of ships, many built especially for this type of
operation and representing the investment of hundreds of millions
of dollars, sail from United States ports. More and more cruises are
originating in the ports of Western Europe, the British Isles, and
Australia, and the demand seems insatiable. Ships of Norwegian
registry dominate the scene, both in the size of the fleet and in the
number of beds offered for sale.2 The vessels themselves, regard
less of nationality, are floating resort hotels, equipped with elabo
rate facilities for passenger recreation, and offering well-pub
licized professional entertainment for the amusement of the
travelers.
Although the enormous popularity of the cruise business is a
relatively recent development, the idea of vacation sea travel can be
traced back more than a century. The first cruise from the United
States was immortalized by Mark Twain in Innocents Abroad. This
humorous masterpiece describes the experiences of a group of
Americans who voyaged to the Mediterranean in the paddle wheel
steamer Quaker City in 1867. It is worth the trouble to read the sales

## PASSENGER CRUISES 377
promotion material quoted by Mark Twain and to compare it with
the modern versions. Only the phraseology has changed; the intent
to allure the prospective passenger with both the glamour and
romance of far-off places and pleasure of the ship has not changed
one whit.
Exactly when cruising began on the other side of the Atlantic is
not readily to be determined. A "special Mediterranean tour" was
operated by the Peninsular & Oriental Steam Navigation Company
for its British customers in 1844. In 1891, Albert Ballin, the guiding
genius of the Hamburg-America Line, sent the Augusta Victoria on a
cruise around the Mediterranean rather than have her sail half
empty on her regular transatlantic route during the winter season.3
Until the disappearance of regular passenger liner service dur
ing the late 1960s and early 1970s, almost all the ships that engaged
in cruising out of United States ports were assigned for the major
portion of the year to shuttle operations between fixed ports. Dur
ing the winter months, when the volume of passenger traffic
dropped to very low levels, it was common practice to divert all but
the very largest liner vessels to cruise operation, even though only a
portion of their accommodations could be considered acceptable
for warm weather travel. In the years antedating World War II, as
many as ninety ships were scheduled for cruises during the colder
months. Year-round cruising was not feasible until air-condition
ing was installed on ships, and passengers could be assured of
comfortable quarters regardless of outdoor temperatures.4
Cunard Line introduced the Caronia in 1949 as a ship intended
primarily for cruising, but adaptable to liner service whenever
there was sufficient demand. This dual purpose design proved
satisfactory, and other steamship owners followed Cunard's lead.
Interchanging ships between liner and cruise service ended when
jet-propelled aircraft began carrying passengers across the oceans.
International steamship service was overwhelmed by the competi
tion, and gradually faded away during the years between 1960 and
1972.
Driven off their regular routes by the airplane, many passenger
liners were placed in year-round cruise service. Newer ships like the
Caronia had been planned for this type of employment, and were
fitted with the amenities demanded by the cruising public: outdoor
swimming pools, broad decks for strolling and playing games, large

## 378 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
public rooms with good views to seaward, complete air-condition
ing, and private baths for every cabin. These vessels made the
transition to their new employment with considerable success.
Some of the older ships were rebuilt completely, while others were
sold for scrap. Reconstruction was justified on two counts: it took
much less time than to build a ship, and it cost about half as much.
As work weeks have been shortened and vacations (or holidays)
have become universal in the industrialized nations, the demand
for recreation has intensified. As early as 1954, Greek shipowners
sensed this and began to offer short voyages from Piraeus to the
nearby islands of the Aegean Sea. These cruises varied in length
from three to seven days and proved immensely popular, affording
employment to two dozen ships:~
Holiday voyages were offered from England, France, West
Germany, Russia, Indonesia, and Australia. Surprisingly, about
one hundred thousand Russians enjoyed the pleasures of a seago
ing vacation. At least one ship, the Gruziya, with space for about 480
passengers, was operated between Crimean and Caucasian resorts.
Russian flag ships also participated for several years in the cruise
trade from the United States, offering rates significantly below
those of the competition. This operation ended in 1979 when the
United States government reacted against the Russion invasion of
Afghanistan and barred the ships from American ports. The
Holland-America Line assigned a ship to Indonesia during the
European winter season, and found it a profitable enterprise when
combined with scheduled airline services bringing passengers from
distant lands to the port of embarkation. Pearl Cruises of Scandina
via since 1981 has based its 425-passenger Pearl of Scandinavia in the
Orient, sailing from Singapore, Hong Kong, and Kobe to ports of
China. Alternate routings took the ship to the Philippines, Thailand,
and other Pacific points.6
West Germany, as the most prosperous nation in Western Eu
rope, presented a very attractive market for cruise operators.
Hapag-Lloyd, the largest German shipowner, bought the Kungs
holm (built for the Swedish American Line in 1952) in 1972, and
under the name of Europa placed her in full-time cruise service. In
1977, bookings for the ship were at the level of 70 percent to 80
percent of capacity; the occupancy rate required to cover expenses
was 63 percent to 64 percent at the fare structure then in effect.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 379
Prices charged for cruises in the Europa were about 30 percent
higher than those of the average cruise ship, but were justified by
the superb condition in which she was maintained, the unceasing
attention of her owners to assuring that the ship met the highest
standards of the maritime authorities, the excellence of her cuisine,
and her outstanding wine cellar, which was said to have been
superior to the best luxury hotels.7
Hapag-Lloyd in November 1978 ordered a replacement for the
aging Europa, awarding the contract to Bremer-Vulkan in West
Germany at a price of $85,000,000. The new ship, also named
Europa, was delivered in 1982. She had accommodations for 600
passengers, an unusually small number for a 27 ,OOO-gross ton ship.
Her design differed from other cruise vessels in that the rather
spacious passenger cabins were in the forward (therefore quieter)
area, and the public rooms were spread over several decks in the
after part. She also had an outdoor pool which could be enclosed by
a glass superstructure. The crew, numbering 275, was quartered in
182 rooms, each with a shower and toilet. Special recreation facili
ties including a swimming pool also were provided for the staff.s
The decision to build a new ship for the cruise trade is of the first
magnitude. Between the signing of the building contract and sail
ing on the first voyage, a period of about three years must elapse.
The owners must be optimistic that the world's economy will en
courage thousands of people to spend millions of dollars (or
pounds sterling, or kroner, or drachmas) on pleasure voyages. This
optimism, although based on the most careful and conservative
forecasts and calculations, still represents an enormous gamble.
Fortunately, the gamble has paid handsome rewards during most
of the years since 1945.
On May 4, 1984, the 46,000-ton Fairsky arrived in Los Angeles
on her delivery voyage from the shipyard at La Seyne sur Mer,
France, bringing to a climax three years of construction and, ac
cording to the San Francisco Examiner of June 10, 1984, ten years of
planning. The owners of the $150 million vessel adopted a novel
procedure when they hired four interior design firms-from En
gland, Finland, Italy, and the United States-to plan different areas
of the ship. While coordination and consultation among these four
were necessary, each one was permitted to carry out individual
schemes for their assigned sections. The American designer, Joszi

## 380 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The Queen Elizabeth 2 (built 1969) was designed for regular transatlantic
service in summer, and cruise operation during the other seasons. She
carried 1,870 passengers; the crew numbered 940. Photo: Cunard Line.
Meskan, commented in an interview that her awareness of the
larger physical dimensions of Americans had influenced the design
of seats throughout the ship. She also remembered that many
American women habitually use hair dryers, and therefore had the
lamps on every dressing table equipped with an outlet for 120-volt
electrical equipment.9
Operating a passenger carrying ship in any trade is expensive,
but cruise ship owners also must pay for the entertainers and social
activity directors who are essential to success. In 1978, for instance,
the Holland-America Line let it be known that the service and
entertainment staffs on its five cruise ships cost over $9 million per
year. IO In 1981, Royal Caribbean Line paid well-known singers and
other entertainers between $1,500 and $3,500 a week for four to
eight shows. On board the Nordic Prince, the cruise director had a
staff of fifteen assistants, described as singers comedians, masters of
ceremony, or simply as good mixers.! I
In regular liner service, operating costs are offset by revenue
from mail, express, and whatever cargo may be carried as well as
from passenger fares. In cruise service, however, only the proceeds
from travelers' tickets are available to defray the expenses of run
ning the ship. The Queen Elizabeth 2, according to a 1978 report,
had a daily operating bill of $130,000, a figure which required a
very large number of passengers to overcome. Because cruising is a

## PASSENGER CRUISES 381
Constructed in 1976, the 20.5-knot Cunard Countess typified the moder
ate-sized, purpose-built cruise ships. She carried 750 passengers. Photo:
Cunard Line.
vacation activity, and therefore subject to many factors conducive
to cancellation or postponement, it is extraordinarily difficult to
predict months in advance the number of persons who will board
the ship on sailing day. If patronage is good, and the voyage is made
exactly as planned without any unpredicted major expenditures,
the cruise can be a financial success. Ticket revenue for the Queen
Elizabeth 2's three-month, round-the-world cruise of 1975
amounted to $15 million. Disbursements came to only $12 Y2 mil
lion. The unfavorable side of the ledger was displayed three years
later, when the ship had to undergo a major overhaul which forced
cancellation of three successive cruises, with consequent loss of
income.I2
As the passenger liners were phased out of scheduled shuttle
service and cruising became a year-round activity, shipowners set-

## 382 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
tled into fixed patterns of operation. Some restricted their business
to a certain area, as did the Norwegian Caribbean Line, which
concentrated on the Caribbean. Other companies chose to establish
what amounted to shuttle voyages between the home port and one
or two ports of call. Still others allocated their vessels to the longer
voyages, perhaps of fourteen to twenty or more days. From this last
group came the ships placed in extended cruises of fifty to one
hundred days. Because the pool of travelers who might be in
terested in such protracted vacations is very limited-for the most
part they are independently wealthy or retired-only about a half
dozen ships were so employed. Possibly the longest round-the
world cruise in recent years was that of Hapag-Lloyd's Europa.
Originating in Germany, the ISS-day, 30,000-nautical mile voyage
terminated in Genoa on June 4, 1984. Although about two thou
sand passengers traveled in the ship, only forty-two persons actu
ally made the entire cruise.13
Beginning about 1970, the major cruise operators shifted their
base of operation from New York to the Florida ports of Miami,
Port Everglades, and Fort Lauderdale. There were two reasons for
this decision: (1) distances to the islands of the Caribbean, the north
coast of South America, and the Panama Canal are comparatively
short, and passengers may be landed in foreign territory very soon
after departing from the United States; and (2) travelers domiciled
in the northeastern states can fly to Florida in a matter of a few
hours and therefore are able to enjoy the tropical climate for the
maximum period. The transfer to the southern ports proved to be
popular with the patrons of both long and short voyages.
Longer cruises, exemplified by those from the east coast of the
United States to the eastern Mediterranean or around the world,
represent a challenge to the managerial skill of the operator while
presenting him with a very real gamble. To win the favor of a
notoriously fickle and highly selective public, the traffic managers
must devise itineraries to allure those planning shipboard vaca
tions. They must determine how many hours ashore will satisfy the
desires of the passengers for sightseeing and shopping, how many
days should be spent at sea between ports so that the travelers will
not become too fatigued, and how long the cruises should last from
start to finish. Their goal is to come as close as possible to the ideal
set forth in this quotation from a column by Georgia Hess in the

## PASSENGER CRUISES 383
travel section of the San Francisco Examiner and Chronicle on Septem
ber 21,1980:
Where does Supership sail? When I'm aboard, it will be in waters
where there are fascinating ports of call for two or three days running,
then a day at sea, then more ports, then another rest day, etc. Others
might prefer the vastness of midocean where day follows day at sea,
allowing adaptation to the rhythm of nature and the leisure to do
nothing but sleep.
When Supership is in port, no other ships are there to disgorge
their hundreds of shop-seekers. Taxis are available as well as tour
buses, but of course Supership always docks within walking distance
of midtown, like on the Grand Canal in Venice.
Supership calls at a judicious combination of tried-and-true des
tinations and rarely visited ports: in the Greek Islands, for instance,
she never skips Santorini, but might add Samothrace.
To meet the expectations of people like the newspaper colum
nist quoted above, the experts begin their planning by canvassing
by mail, by telephone, and in person the travel agents who are the
best producers of cruise business, have the greatest contact with the
travelers, and therefore know what their current desires may be.
From these analyses of the travel trade and the reactions of their
patrons to experiences on cruises, surprisingly accurate data may
be obtained. Additional information may be procured from resort
hotels and conventions of business and professional associations
where wealthy travelers congregate. Assessing all these bits and
pieces of knowledge, the cruise manager can determine rather
definitely what will be acceptable to the public.
Occasionally, even the most highly qualified experts are frus
trated by events altogether beyond the control of ship operators.
Incidents in the recent past illustrate the variety of these
circumstances.
In late 1973, the Greek Line proposed to use its Olympia for a
series of week-long voyages from Piraeus to eastern Mediterranean
ports. The first trip was scheduled for early March 1974. As the
New Year dawned, friction between Israel and its Arab neighbors
was so threatening that the cruise program had to be cancelled, and
for want of other employment the Olympia was laid up.14
Eastern Steamship Lines, operators of the steamer Bahama Star
in cruises between Florida and the Bahama Islands, announced in

## 384 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
January 1975 that it was terminating the service because of the
extremely high cost of fuel oil and foodstuffs. Whereas in 1973 only
150 passengers were needed to pay expenses of the voyage, by the
end of 1974 it was necessary to carry at least 350 to break even. The
ship was laid up following the owners' determination that the
required quota of travelers could not be found every week. IS
The Oriental Empress, owned by C. Y. Tung, of Hong Kong, left
Los Angeles on December 7, 1973, on a projected one-hundred
day cruise around the world. The international crisis resulting
from fuel shortages caused abrupt cancellation of the voyage when
the ship reached Hong Kong on January 8, 1974. The ship had
exhausted her supply of oil, all efforts made en route to obtain fuel
having been unsuccessful. Her passengers were flown back to the
United States, and the unused portion of their ticket money was
refunded. 16
When England undertook military action in the Falkland Is
lands in April 1982, the P & 0 Line's cruise ship Canberra and the
Cunard Line's Queen Elizabeth 2 were requistioned on very short
notice to serve as troop carriers for an indefinite period. Both ships
had been scheduled for extensive cruise operations, and thousands
of would-be passengers suddenly had to make other plans.I7 The
Costa Line, an Italian carrier which annually made a cruise to
Argentina and included a call at the Falkland Islands, cancelled the
voyage of the Eugenio less than a week before her scheduled de
parture because of "uncertainties related to the Falkland Islands
crisis." 18
Competition for the available patronage is very keen. Traffic
departments constantly seek ways to induce new segments of the
general public to embark on cruises. When the seven-day voyage
became a standard offering of the industry, many persons who
could not take longer vacations discovered that a shipboard holiday
was a genuine possibility. Others who had been interested in cruises
but had been deterred both by the high daily rate and the length of
the voyages found that they could afford the shorter trips. To
attract those who had evidenced no previous enthusiasm for these
"pleasure jaunts," promoters developed "special interest cruises,"
with a wide variety of inducements: a jazz festival, with a well
known artist as the principal exponent; an educational activity
featuring a prominent scholar; an investment forum and symposi-

## PASSENGER CRUISES 385
Top: The Sea Goddess I and her twin, Sea Goddess II, accommodate only 116
passengers in unusual luxury. Bottom: The dining room of the Sea God
dess ships. Photos: Sea Goddess Cruises.

## 386 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Top: Passengers' main lounge aboard the Sea Goddess l. Bottom: The
reception area of the Sea Goddess I is enhanced by soft, indirect lighting
and Persian rugs. Photos: Sea Goddess Cruises.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 387
ium conducted by a team of financial counsellors; an athletic collo
quium chaired by world famous sports coaches and commentators;
a musical festival centered on a distinguished symphony orchestra;
and a gourmet's special emphasizing various national cuisines. Per
haps the most adventurous innovation in cruise incentives was to
invite 98 persons to take part in the first passenger ship voyage
through the Northwest Passage. Sailing from St. John'S, New
foundland, Canada, on August 20, 1984, the Lindblad Explorer
completed the voyage through these northern waters on Septem
ber 12. Only 35 ships have traversed this route across the northern
rim of Canada since 1906. Capitalizing upon the successful out
come of the 1984 venture, a larger ship, the World Discoverer,
carrying 140 passengers, sailed on August 23, 1985 from Nome,
Alaska, to Halifax, Nova Scotia, on what was said to be the first
eastbound crossing of the Northwest Passage. The trip was com
pleted successfully on September 14.19
Shore excursions are made available, usually at extra cost, to
passengers on almost all cruises. Normally, the shipowner specifi
cally and categorically disavows any connection with or responsibil
ity for these sightseeing trips, and all its promotional literature
contains carefully worded statements to that effect. It is true, how
ever, that the disavowal is somewhat technical, in that the ship
owner nearly always has selected a single concessionaire at each
port of call to provide the tours, tickets for which are sold on board
the cruise vessel. The alternative is to select a travel agency with a
worldwide organization and have it responsible for all shore trips.
The shipowner, in either case, must assure that the contractor or
concessionaire performs to specification. Obviously it does the ship
owner's reputation no good if passengers are displeased by their
experiences ashore. After all, they do not have the opportunity to
choose among the competing excursion operators. In realization of
this ultimate managerial burden, steamship companies exercise
great care in the selection and appointment of concessionaires.
An interesting exception to the general rule that shore excur
sions are optional at extra cost was observed during 1980 in the
voyages of the motorship Aquamarine, which sailed fortnightly from
Hong Kong or Kobe on cruises to China. Because of restrictions
imposed by the government of China, only those passengers who
had made reservations for shore trips were allowed to leave the ship

## 388 TH E B USINESS OF SHIPPI NG
All fifty-eight suites in the Sea Goddess I are identical. Photo: Sea Goddess
Cruises.
for any purpose. Shore excursions therefore were made an integral
part of the cruise "package."
Sun Line, advertising the Stella Maris' two projected fourteen
day "grand gala" cruises in the Mediterranean during the fall of
1985, announced that the voyage would offer thirty-five ancient
sights "with all land arrangements complimentary." Pearl Cruises
of Scandinavia, as did the Aquamarine, included the cost of "nonop
tional" shore excursions in China.
Just as ships engaged in liner service were designed expressly to
meet the needs of a particular route and clientele, so vessels in
tended for cruise operation must be planned to meet the demands
of this specialized employment. Whether an existing ship is to be
purchased, or a new ship is to be built, the prospective owners must
fix definitely the trade to which the ship will be assigned, and the
special features needed to fit her for this assignment. The Norwe
gian Caribbean Line, for instance, established to its satisfaction that
there was a good opportunity to operate a very large ship in short

## PASSENGER CRUISES 389
When named in 1980, the Norway was the world's largest cruise ship,
accommodating 2,400 passengers. Her crew numbered 800. Photo: Jeff
Blinn, courtesy Moran Towing Co.
cruises from Florida to the Virgin Islands. The superliner France
was available for purchase and seemed to meet the ideas of the
potential buyers. Exhaustive studies were conducted to determine
the cruising speed desirable on this route, the adaptations and
modifications essential if the passenger accommodations were to be
acceptable in the subtropical environment, and the earning capabil
ity of the ship. The computations indicated that the proposal to
acquire the France and put her into the seven day cruise service was
economically sound. In June 1979, ownership was transferred
from the Compagnie Generale Maritime, of Paris, to the Norwe
gian Caribbean Line. A multimillion dollar conversion, which in
