cluded changing the ship's name to Norway, preceded her entry in

## 390 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
May 1980 into year-round cruise operation. The wisdom of the
decision to acquire the largest cruise ship in the world has been
borne out by the owners' experience.2o
The Royal Viking Line, on the contrary, required more flexibil
ity and long-distance sailing capability from the three ships it built.
Annually, at least one of these vessels was assigned to a long,
perhaps a world-circling, cruise. The ships therefore were designed
with large fuel tanks, adequate distillation equipment to provide all
the fresh water desired by the passengers, and accommodations
that would be comfortable regardless of the climatic zone in which
the ship happened to be. Their draft assured that they could enter
practically any harbor in the world, from well-known commercial
meccas like Hong Kong and Copenhagen to the out-of-the-way
islands of Majuro in the Marshalls and Saipan in the Marianas, and
to the ever popular Hamilton, Bermuda.
Built and operated to cater to very wealthy passengers who
demand (and will pay for) the utmost in luxury and personal
service, the Sea Goddess I and Sea Goddess II carry 116 passengers on
seven- and fourteen-day cruises from base ports to off-the-beaten
track and relatively unknown harbors in the Mediterranean and
Caribbean seas, and along the Atlantic coast of South America.
Delivered by Finland's Wartsila shipyard in 1984 and 1985, the
ships cost $34,000,000 each, equivalent to $293,100 per passenger
bed-the most expensive cruise ships ever built. Instead of cabins,
the ships offered 58 two-room suites, complete with full bath and
equipped with remote control television, radio, stereophonograph,
videocassette recorder, refrigerator, and bar stocked with liquor
chosen by the occupants. In 1985, a uniform rate of $3,600 per
person, double occupancy, was charged for Mediterranean cruises.
The rate was increased in 1986 to $4,000 for the seven-day cruises.
Every passenger was considered a guest, so no charges of any sort
were made for personal services or beverages at the bar. Tipping
was discouraged. Meals were served at the convenience of the
passengers, and every dish was prepared individually.21
The owners' advertisement offered these inducements to those
who could afford to indulge their tastes:
A cruise ship that takes exception to all the cliches and regi
mented activities of traditional cruises, she has the ambiance of a regal
yacht, the amenities of a stable liner, ...

## PASSENGER CRUISES 391
Long before you sail, the Captain will write to ask your prefer
ences in every detail, from the competitive sports you want in your
visits ashore to the complimentary spirits you wish in your personal
bar.
And the moment you come aboard, you will sense that a Sea
Goddess is indeed a different world. The Persian carpets warm your
welcome. The European staff knows your name ...
Come live the Sea Goddess life.
Selecting the ports to which a cruise ship is to be sent is a very
demanding task. There must be enough of the popular cities to
satisfy both first time and well-experienced travelers, while there
also must be new places which will stimulate the interest of those
who have patronized this carrier on earlier cruises. For short voy
ages off the Adantic coast of the United States, the favorite destina
tions are Bermuda, the Virgin Islands, the Netherlands Antilles,
and Trinidad-Tobago. Long cruises have been most successful
when the itinerary includes the traditional attractions of Bangkok,
Capetown, Casablanca, Copenhagen, Hong Kong, Honolulu, and
Sydney.
An interesting trend in the sales management of cruises
emerged about 1975. Instead of stressing the glamourous nature of
the ports of call, increased emphasis was given to the luxury and
comfort of the ships, the pleasures to be enjoyed in passage from
one harbor to another, the professional entertainment provided
each evening at sea, the varied nature of shipboard activities avail
able to passengers, and the elegance of the meals served in dining
rooms. The unique feature of the cruise-a vacation afloat while
living in a deluxe hotel-became a major selling point, and if the
number of persons embarking on cruise ships each year was any
criterion, it was what the traveling public wanted.
The ship which, because she was unique and also because the
luxury of her accommodation possibly exceeded that of any com
mercial vessel afloat, epitomized the appeal of the ship herself
rather than any itinerary, was the glamourous Sea Cloud. Built in
1931 as a four-masted, steel-hulled bark, this magnificent yacht
embodied every feature that exquisite taste and unlimited wealth
could provide. She was 316 feet long, and had a beam of 49 feet,
and was the largest square-rigged private yacht ever constructed.
When she was rebuilt in 1979, at a cost of six and a half million
dollars, the Sea Cloud's passenger capacity was expanded from the

## 392 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The beautiful barque-rigged Sea Cloud has thirty-one sails. She is unique
in the world's cruise ship fleet for her oceangoing capability as well as for
the luxury of her passenger quarters. Photo: Travel Dynamics.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 393
original twenty-three berths in two owner's suites and eleven other
rooms by adding twenty-six double and two single cabins, while
retaining the standards ofluxury set by the first owner. In 1985, she
was employed in Mediterranean cruises.
Some idea of the sales appeal of the ship may be gained from
these paragraphs in a recent advertising circular:
The original owner's suites and staterooms present features and
ambience not found on any other ship. Large and comfortable, they
are wood-paneled, featuring antique furniture, fireplace and Italian
marble bathroom. The new cabins were constructed in keeping with
the original style. Although somewhat smaller, they are equally com
fortable. The dining room, in the manner of a Scottish country-house,
and the adjoining lounge with its beautiful wood-carved walls, to
gether accommodate all passengers at one sitting. The lounge also
serves as a lecture room. The food and service aboard are equal to the
ship's elegance with complimentary wine served at every meal. The
ship is managed by fifty-five skilled German officers and crew. Life
aboard is relaxed and informal, and passengers are welcome to help
with the operation of the sails. There is plenty of open space for
sunning and relaxing.
The Sea Cloud is a ship that meets the highest safety standards.
With a hull constructed of steel, the vessel is classified by German
Lloyd as lOOA-4 Yacht - the highest classification. Large sums of
money have been spent to install the latest safety equipment which
includes fire detectors and automatic sprinkler fire-fighting systems
in all staterooms and public areas.
The Sea Cloud is unique in every respect. Stately, elegant, and
comfortable, she provides a cruising experience which, until now, was
available only to a few privileged individuals.
Regardless of the wonders of the ship carrying the cruising
public, it remains true that the itinerary must include places that
have the power to intrigue the fancy of the travelers. Promotional
literature still features the exotic nature of foreign cities and the
fascination of the areas the shore excursions will explore. It is no
accident that, from the days of Mark Twain to the present, the
brightness of tropical stars, the brilliance of phosphorescent seas,
the magic of the unceasing trade winds, and the glamour of far-off
places continue to be extolled.
The lure of the unusual, in contradistinction to the sybaritic
luxury of the ship, was exploited to the full by at least one promoter.

## 394 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Built as a yacht to suit the taste of a multimillionaire, the owner's suite of
Sea Cloud was spacious and sumptuously outfitted. Today, this suite is the
most luxurious accommodation offered by a ship in commercial service.
Photo: Travel Dynamics.
Lindblad Travel, Inc., built a ship of2,481 gross registered tons in
1969 to carry 98 passengers to truly out-of-the-ordinary destina
tions. Voyages were made, for example, to the Palmer Peninsula in
Antarctica, the Galapagos Islands, and Easter Island. In 1972, the
Lindblad Explorer was sold to a subsidiary of the Swedish American
Line, and in 1985 was purchased by an American enterprise, Soci
ety Expeditions, Inc., which continued the program of unusual
voyages.
Peak demand for cruises, both in the United States and in
Western Europe, occurs during the winter months when those who
can arrange vacations seek respite from the rigors of cold weather
and its attendant discomforts. In recent years, shipowners serving
the United States cruise market have found it advantageous to base
their ships in southern ports: Miami and Fort Lauderdale are
favored on the Atlantic Coast, and Los Angeles is preferred on the
Pacific Coast. Several steamship operators run their ships out of

## PASSENGER CRUISES 395
San Juan, Puerto Rico. On the other side of the world, the Holland
America Line stations a ship in Indonesia in a successful attempt to
cater to a developing clientele. Passengers fly from their homes to
the embarkation port, thereby spending the maximum number of
days in the warm weather area.
Selection of a base port from which cruise ships will operate is
conditioned primarily on the convenience with which passengers
may be transported to that point. For decades, almost all cruises
from the United States Adantic coast originated in New York, in
large measure because of the network of railroads which fed that
metropolis. The obvious drawback to New York was that a winter
voyage to the Caribbean always began and ended with at least
twenty-four hours of steaming through cold weather and often
stormy seas. When high-speed jet-propelled aircraft put all cities
east of the Mississippi River within two to four hours flying time
from Miami, the picture changed dramatically. Most of the ships
serving the Caribbean area were assigned to the Florida ports of
Miami, Port Everglades, and Fort Lauderdale. An incidental bene
fit to the shipowners was that the airplane widened enormously the
region from which cruise patronage might be obtained. It is no
longer remarkable that residents of California are among those
embarking on one-or two-week cruises from Florida.
Economic and geographical logic, however, do not always pro
duce the result envisioned by traffic managers. In 1974, the Royal
Viking Line determined that midwest and southeastern United
States customers would find New Orleans attractive as a port from
which to start their cruises. The Royal Viking Star was selected for
this service, and during the three winter seasons from 1974 to 1977
a series of cruises was operated from this city. The response of the
passengers was disappointing despite excellent airline connections
and schedules, and the attraction of the city itself. At the beginning
of the 1977-1978 fall cruise season, the ship was returned to the
base port of Fort Lauderdale, from which it operated with greater
success.
A significant expansion of this alliance between airlines and
cruise ships occurred about 1970. Passengers who desired to spend
twelve of their fourteen days of vacation cruising through the
Mediterranean or to points along the North and Baltic Sea coasts
flew from all parts of the world to international gateways like

## 396 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
London, Marseilles, and Athens. In these cities, they made connec
tions with surface transportation directly to the sides of the ships
which would take them on their cruises. Beginning in 1981, the
round-the-world cruise of the Queen Elizabeth 2 was offered in
whole or part, depending on the desires of the traveler. The prac
tice quickly spread, and identical inducements were offered by
competing operators. Cunard countered by making special ar
rangements by which travelers in the Queen Elizabeth 2 would be
flown by supersonic jet aircraft from designated cities to seaports
where they would join the ship. The cost of air travel was included,
in whole or part, in the cruise price, or was deducted from the ship's
fare.
Because of the keen competition between cruise operators, the
most careful attention is given to advertising, promoting, and sail
ing individual cruises. In the case of the ships which, like the
Norway, sail every week on a fixed route, the emphasis is placed
upon the ship and the ports of call, rather than on any particular
date. If the ship is assigned to a long cruise, such as a voyage around
the world, the unusual features of the trip receive special stress.
The Cunard Line's folder describing the world cruise for 1986
contained these words:
Discover new parts of the world on the most talked about ship of
our time-Queen Elizabeth 2.
You'll experience a round-the-world voyage never before ac
complished-in the true spirit of the great adventurers. The QE2 will
visit 32 enchanting ports on 4 continents, including 9 maiden arrivals.
Sail ... to the tip of South America, transit the Strait of Magellan (a
world cruise first), and explore the coasts of Africa, Asia, and the
Indian subcontinent.
And all in the unparalleled splendor of the QE2.
Longer cruises represent a special effort on the part of the
operator, and in most cases are planned for a selected ship and a
particular time. The names of the ships and their owners often
serve to attract patrons; either these travelers are "repeaters," who
have made previous cruises with the ship or the company, or they
are persons who have been impressed with the reputation of the
operator. Often the reservation books are opened twelve to fifteen
months before the departure date; always there is as much publicity
and advertising as can be arranged to attract attention and to
provide impetus to the sales campaign.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 397
To establish some rules and thereby to forestall cutthroat com
petition, thirteen cruise ship operators came together in 1970 to
form the Caribbean Cruise Association. As was true of the confer
ences established in other trades, the aim of this group was to adopt
a code of ethics concerning promotion and sale of cruise services,
and to fix certain areas of responsibility for the welfare of the
traveling public. Five years later, after an interim merger in 1972
into the International Passenger Ship Association, the Cruise Lines
International Association came into existence with administrative
offices in New York and San Francisco. This body consisted of the
twenty-five major cruise ship operators catering to the tourist trade
from the United States, functioned in a manner similar to other
steamship conferences, and was subject to the scrutiny of the Fed
eral Maritime Commission by reason of its relation to American
foreign commerce.
The more desirable the accommodation aboard ship, the higher
the cost to the person taking the cruise. The most modern ships in
1981 featured cabins which, by categories, were almost identical in
size, decor, facilities, and amenities. About three-quarters of all
rooms were "outside," with portholes looking out to sea. Suites and
oversize rooms, which could be combined to form a suite, were in
the best locations. Inside rooms cost less than outside, and double
rooms were priced lower than single-bedded quarters. While the
newest ships provided a bath for every room, tubs were available
only by paying a premium rate; the standard bathrooms offered
only a shower. The older ships had many cabins without a bath; a
substantial increase in the fare was demanded for any room with
private bath.
Indicative of the differentials charged for space in a modern
ship designed with "modular," i.e., standardized, accommodations
were the following categories of rooms. The rates shown are for
each person, and are listed below by categories ranging downward
from the most desirable rooms in the ship (the suites) to the least
desirable cabins, located on the waterline deck:
I A suite, consisting of a bedroom
with twin beds, separate sitting
room, and tub/shower bath, on
the promenade deck, looking
forward Highest price

## 39R THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
II A suite, similar to the above but
without the view forward; also
located on the promenade deck $500 less than I
III Deluxe single room, outside; all
decks except the promenade
deck $280 less than II
IV Twin bed room, outside, with
shower, on promenade deck $182 less than III
V Twin bed room, outside, with
shower, one deck lower than
rooms in Category IV $196 less than IV
VI Twin bed room, outside, with
shower, two decks below Catego-
ry IV space (one deck above wa-
terline deck) $126 less than V
VII Twin bed room, outside, with
shower, waterline deck $56 less than VI
VIII Room with double bed, outside,
with shower; all decks except
promenade deck $70 less than VII
IX Room with double bed, inside,
with shower, two top decks $238 less than VIII
X Room with double bed, inside,
with shower, same deck as Cate-
gory VI $126 less than IX
XI Room with double bed, inside,
with shower, waterline deck $42 less than X
Modular construction, as noted above, was common on many of
the newest ships. This prompted one operator to adopt the practice
of making reservations for beds in a category of rooms rather than
in a room selected by the passenger at the time of booking. The
advertising folder issued by this operator contained this statement:
The modular design of the ships [in our fleet] provides for a standard
of amenities and quality by deck location within each room type.
These ships are equipped with stabilizers to provide maximum stabil
ity in all cabins. You will receive a confirmation of the room type of
your choice at the time of booking. Specific stateroom number will be
assigned at the time of ticketing or shortly prior to embarkation.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 399
Seating capacity in the ship's dining room frequently was
limited, and two sittings were required to handle the total number
of embarked passengers. There is a perennial argument about the
desirability of the "first sitting" or the "second sitting." Those
travelers who have children or who follow early schedules usually
elected the earlier hours; others who considered that dining at late
hours somehow reflected greater social status, or who normally led
lives starting later in the day, chose the second sitting. The food was
identical, but might be a bit fresher for the first serving. There was
some pressure on diners to finish their meals and to leave the dining
room to be prepared for the later group. The Queen Elizabeth 2 was
designed as a three class, shuttle-service passenger liner, and had
three different dining rooms. Fares were keyed to both the desir
ability of the individual rooms and the dining room to which cruise
passengers were assigned. There was an inherent but undeniable
"snob appeal" about having a table reservation in the same room
and at the same time as the passengers occupying the highest priced
suites.
In addition to the revenue from the sale of tickets, cruise
operators looked to the ship's bar (or bars, in many cases) and shops
for significant contributions to voyage profits. On short trips, bar
profits could make the difference between a break-even operation
and a profitable voyage. On long trips, where there was a great deal
of social activity and many private parties, the bar profits could be
substantial. Shops of all sorts became more and more prominent
aboard cruise ships. The "International Promenade" on the Norway
had seven shops offering luxury goods at prices rivalling those in
the most exclusive stores ashore. Jewelry, porcelain, watches, cloth
ing, sportswear, and children's clothing and toys were featured. An
exotic "boutique" offered Chinese silk robes, jade flowers, carved
ivory goddesses, and exquisite little tea sets. Passengers were lured
into these places by their presence and also by the fact that on the
high seas there were no duties on imported goods. (Whatever was
purchased at sea had to be declared to customs officials, and might
be subjected to duty when brought into the United States if the
value exceeded the "free allowance" accorded by the government
to returning residents.)
Some ship operators have elected to contract with specialists to
handle all details connected with the stores. If this were done, the

## 400 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
concessionaire procured the merchandise, hired the necessary sales
personnel, maintained the required inventories, and assured that
stocks were replenished before the commencement of each voyage.
Usually the contract required that the seagoing sales-persons be
quartered and subsisted either as cruise staff members or at the
same level as junior officers of the ship.
To reduce the operational responsibility of the shipowner
while making certain that qualified personnel were assigned to
those duties, some shipowners made contracts with food-service
organizations to take over the entire catering activity of the cruise
ship. When this was done, the contractor agreed to purchase,
deliver, store, prepare, and serve all food during the cruise, and to
hire, train, and supervise the dining room waiters and helpers and
the necessary kitchen staff in its many specialized subdivisions; to
plan and execute appropriate menus for each day; to be ready to
meet individual orders by passengers for parties of any and every
kind; and to operate the crew's galley in all particulars. A fixed fee
per passenger per day might be charged, or the arrangement might
call for a service fee over and above the actual cost of all purchased
stores. The contractor assigned a person considered qualified to
manage this complex operation who was responsible to senior
management ashore for satisfactory performance under the con
tract. The shipboard representative of the owners who supervised
the contractor's activities usually was the ship's purser. One com
pany took a new approach to solving the problem of good supervi
sion and placed on each of its ships a highly qualified specialist
known as the hotel manager. He had exactly the responsibilities
that his title suggested, and he reported directly to the master of the
ship. Although this violated the traditional position of the purser, it
accomplished two objectives: it permitted the purser to concentrate
upon the activities peculiar to his office by relieving him of major
distractions from the efficient operation of his department. It also
assured that the standards of service to cruise passengers would
equal, at the minimum, those of the finest hotels ashore.
The practice of bringing concessionaires aboard ship to operate
the dining rooms and bars did not meet with universal approval.
Many complaints were registered by passengers, who stated that
the personnel provided by the contractor often were poorly
trained, inefficient, and ungracious in manner. Maritime labor

## PASSENGER CRUISES 401
unions alleged that the concessionaires procured catering staff on
the basis of the least possible cost, without regard to the capabilities
of those workers. They also charged that the treatment of these
persons was unsatisfactory, in that they were crowded into inade
quate quarters and required to work excessively long hours.22
Whatever the truth might be, it must be acknowledged that
using concessionaires places the reputation of the shipowner in
serious jeopardy. In the first place, passengers assume that every
one working aboard the ship is employed directly by the steamship
operator, and therefore condemn the company for any shortcom
ings or mistakes. In the second place, the concessionaire may have
experience in catering ashore, but not in shipboard activity. Unless
the owner has a large and very well trained staff of supervisors to
keep the concessionaire's performance up to the desired or stipu
lated standard, there can be no certainty that the promises made by
the traffic department will be carried out. In the third place, the
contractor does all the purchasing and inspection of foodstuffs,
and the quality of what is procured may not be ascertained by the
ship's representatives until it is offered to the passengers. If the
meat or vegetables are of inferior grade, the criticism levied against
the vessel could have serious effects upon the efforts of the traffic
department to find customers.
A few cruise operators have attempted to capitalize on the
popularity of casino gambling by installing full-scale casinos aboard
their ships. To avoid any conflicts with territorial laws governing
gambling, the seagoing casinos were open only when the vessel was
in international waters.23 Professional personnel to staff the casinos
in some instances were recruited by the shipowners from the ranks
of those working in casinos ashore; in other cases, the whole opera
tion was placed under contract to a concessionaire who shared the
profits on an agreed basis. On January 15, 1985, the New York
Journal of Commerce reported that a state-controlled company in
Austria had active contracts to operate the casinos aboard nineteen
cruise ships around the world. The magnitude of each ship's gam
bling activity may be gauged by the fact that the Royal Caribbean
Cruise Line's facilities, which were handled by this Austrian conces
sionaire, comprised eight blackjack tables, an Austrian roulette
table, and one hundred computerized slot machines.
Adding this type of amusement to the recreational program of

## 402 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
the cruise ships was acclaimed by many travelers, but it had to be
regarded by the ship operators with great caution. Regardless of
whether the casino were run by the ship or by the concessionaire,
the reputation of the carrier always was in jeopardy. Complaints by
irate losers could become explosive and probably would become the
subject of gossip among cruise passengers, to the ultimate detri
ment of the steamship company. Supervision of all gambling activ
ity therefore must be exemplary, and the fact of constant surveil
lance given publicity in advance of any possible complaints.
For those operators who send their ships on a variety of voyages,
some long and some short, some repeating earlier experiences and
some new in many respects, the normal procedure is for the traffic
department to conjure up the itinerary that will be most attractive
to the public, and then to have the marine operations department
determine what is feasible, given the characteristics and capabilities
of the ship. Of major importance is the depth of water available in
the suggested ports of call; unless it exceeds the draft of the ship, it
will be necessary to bring passengers ashore in tenders. If tenders
are required, local conditions of wind, surf, height of waves, and
landing area must be scrutinized. If the ship is to be anchored some
distance offshore, and a heavy swell is experienced throughout the
year, it means that passengers may have to jump into the tender
when the swell is at the peak. This requires both agility and good
timing; serious injury can result from hesitation or lost footing. In
all probability, the recommendation from the operations depart
ment would be to delete that place from the itinerary.
Each proposed port of call is given the same methodical exami
nation, and only those which are acceptable from the viewpoint of
the navigators and ship handlers are approved. The traffic depart
ment must adapt its plans to the conditions discovered by the
operating department. Once the ports of call are set, the engineer
ing department works out the schedule of the ship's movements to
achieve maximum economy and efficiency. Speed is of relatively
minor importance to cruise passengers, but is of great significance
to management. Slower speeds require very much less fuel than the
maximum speed of which the vessel is capable. Whenever speed can
be reduced without thereby increasing the duration of the voyage
to an uneconomical point, it is standard practice to slow the ship.
While most of the purpose-built ships have speeds of approxi
mately 22 to 23 knots, these maximum levels are reached only

## PASSENGER CRUISES 403
occasionally. One itinerary for a round-the-world cruise of a 23-
knot ship required a single leg of sixty-two hours' duration when
the speed was 21.7 knots; all other legs were accomplished at lower
(and hence more economical) speeds ranging between 15 and 20
knots.
Certain basic principles apply to making cruise schedules. One
is that the ship should arrive either at the anchorage, if tenders are
to be used, or at the berth, if wharf or pier is available, not earlier
than eight o'clock in the morning. Time for the passengers to enjoy
the attractions ashore must be proportioned to the physical size of
the place being visited and the interest it may have for tourists.
Generally speaking, an average stay of four hours for an island like
Saipan has been found satisfactory; for a well-known city with
numerous attractions like Honolulu or Copenhagen, a call of at
least sixteen hours is proper. In a city where shopping is the great
lure, as in the case ofSt. Thomas (Virgin Islands), a full business day
must be allowed for the pleasure of the passengers.
Departure time poses something of a problem because of the
possibility of stragglers. Standard procedure is to notify all mem
bers of the cruise party that the ship will sail at the appointed hour,
and anyone who is not on board will have to find his or her way to
the next port of call at personal expense. This notice is published in
the advertising leaflets, the ship's newspaper, and on bulletin
boards in the ship. It is accepted practice in the cruise industry to fix
the exact time to leave a port to accommodate the ship's arrival at
her next scheduled stop. If the distance to be covered is short, as
from San Juan, Puerto Rico, to Christiansted, St. Croix (Virgin
Islands)-104 miles-departure time might be as late as 2:00 A.M.,
and cruising speed would be set at 17 knots to assure arrival at eight
o'clock that same morning.
As already mentioned, the cost of ship's fuel is so great that only
rarely is a cruise ship operated at full throttle. Instead, the lowest
speed that will meet requirements is dictated. From Barcelona,
Spain, to New York, for instance, is 3,728 miles. At 22 knots, this
distance can be traversed in seven days and two hours; at 20 knots,
in seven days and nineteen hours; and at 19 knots, in eight days and
five hours. Very likely, the decision would favor 20 knots, which
would permit departure at 7:00 P.M. and arrival at 2:00 P.M., an
acceptable hour for the end of the cruise.
Ideally, arrival at every port of call would be in the early morn-

## 404 THE BUS I N E S S 0 F S HIP PIN G
ing and departure would be effected after dark, so that passengers
might spend the entire day ashore. In the real world of winds, tides,
and distances, however, the schedule must be made to fit the cir
cumstances regnant in the port at the time of the proposed call. If
tides regulate the hours of entry and departure, or pilots take ships
strictly in the order in which they have arrived at the pilot station,
the schedule must take these facts into account.
Once the itinerary and schedule for the cruise have been de
termined, the supply division of the shipowner organization begins
its work. The deck and engineering departments of the ship must
requisition enough materiel and spare parts for every reasonably
foreseeable need, and must make sure that delivery schedules can
and will be met. This is particularly critical where the ship is com
pleting one fairly long cruise and is to start on another of equal
duration within a maximum of twenty-four hours after arrival.
The catering (or hotel, as it sometimes is called) department
ITlust order the foodstuffs that will be required for the cruise, based
upon the menus which have been prepared. Some appreciation of
the quantities involved may be gleaned from the following report of
purchases made for the Fairsky, a 1,200-passenger cruise ship,
assigned to a ten-day voyage from Los Angeles to the "Mexican
Riviera":
15,186 pounds of meat 22,200 pounds of assorted fresh fruit
3,547 pounds oflobster 9,500 pounds of flour
2,720 pounds of assorted prawns 2,700 pounds of assorted pasta
1,150 pounds of crabmeat 960 gallons of fresh milk
4,242 pounds of assorted fish 1,960 cans of assorted fruits
1R ,600 pounds of fresh vegetables 7,900 pounds of poultry
4,850 poundsoffrozen vegetables 1,800 pounds of coffee
7,900 pounds of potatoes 1,460 pounds of sugar
Bar supplies were ordered in similar large quantities, as evi
denced by this list:
286 bottles of French champagne 38 bottles of gin
690 bottles of American wine 27 bottles of rum
215 bottles of French wine 155 bottles of vodka
585 bottles of Italian wine 95 bottles of assorted liqueurs
40 bottles of cognac 3,940 bottles of assorted beers
60 bottles of whiskey 5,976 bottles of soft drinks
55 bottles of bourbon and rye whiskies

## PASSENGER CRUISES 405
When the France made a round-the-world cruise lasting ninety
one days (from January 10 to April 10, 1972), her executive chef
promised that there would be no repetition of the same recipe.
Fresh vegetables, fresh meat, and fish were procured at various
ports of call, and advance notice of what would be required had to
be furnished to the local purveyors so that they could assemble the
designated quantity of each item. Wines and spirits of all types and
vintages also were stocked against the demands of the passengers.
Although it is an extreme case because of the length of the
cruise and the approximately 1,200 passengers carried, the manner
in which laundry was handled for the France is a lesson in itself.
It was impractical to put aboard the ship enough napkins,
tablecloths, bedspreads, sheets, and towels for ninety-one days. To
keep the accumulation of soiled linen in bags stowed in closets
would have created serious housekeeping problems. The solution
was to ship ahead to strategically located ports of call quantities of
clean linen, which could be exchanged for the soiled items. The
huge collection of dirty linen was to be sent back to France in a
connecting cargo ship. At the same ports, city laundry facilities
were placed under contract to perform the well-nigh impossible. In
Rio de Janeiro, for instance, about ninety thousand pieces of laun
dry were washed, dried, ironed, and returned to the ship within
twenty-four hours, while a simultaneous exchange of clean for
dirty was made of an additional 150,000 pieces. At Valparaiso,
Bombay, and Capetown, similar arrangements were made.24
While some operators believe that the seven-day cruise is the
"bread and butter" of the industry,25 others have put their trust in
the three- and four-day voyages which have become popular in the
United States. Whatever the length of the voyage, the pattern of
operation has become standardized: depart in the early evening,
and return to the home port no later than midmorning. Only eight
to twelve hours therefore are available to make the vessel ready for
the next sailing. It takes little imagination to comprehend the
coordination required between the management staff ashore, the
suppliers, the service agencies located in the port city or its im
mediate environs, and the shipboard personnel. A careful schedule
of deliveries must be set up, and rigidly adhered to; adequate
inspection personnel must be available to assure that what is being
put aboard the ship is what the purchase contract called for, and in
the quantity ordered. The problem is complicated by the fact that

## 406 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
after the ship has been engaged in this seven-day voyage routine for
several months, there will be some rotation of key personnel every
time the vessel comes into port. The arrangements are made some
what more difficult in those instances where the ship's turnaround
day is Sunday, which means that everyone is on the overtime
payroll, and some emergency procurement may be impeded be
cause the supplier has closed for the weekend.
No matter how capacious the tanks may be, a ship sooner or
later will have to be refueled. Petroleum supplies have been trou
blesome and expensive to procure ever since 1973, and the greatest
care and attention must be given to placing orders that assure the
commitments will be honored. The quality of oil used by diesel
powered ships has deteriorated since 1973, and shipboard engi
neers must be alert to test that which is to be pumped into the tanks
to ascertain that it meets minimum specifications. Scheduled time
in those ports where fuel is purchased must be long enough to
permit the oil to be pumped aboard.
An important function of the operating department is to ar
range to bring the ship into port and to care for her until she
departs. That means a pilot must be waiting to direct the cruise
vessel without delay to a waiting berth, for passengers do not accept
apologies when they are delayed in getting ashore. Tugs must be
available to berth and unberth the ship promptly on schedule
unless she is fitted (as many of the newly constructed cruise ships
are) with bow thrusters to facilitate maneuvering without assist
ance. Launches or tenders, if needed to ferry passengers to and
from shore, have to be at the ship's side when the anchor splashes
into the water. Since cruise vessels normally are accorded "yacht
privileges" in the ports they visit, the formalities of entrance and
clearance are reduced to a minimum. It is important, however, that
the ship's officers be informed concerning all local regulations so
that no embarrassment will be experienced by the passengers.
These responsibilities are among those entrusted in each port to the
ship's agent, who must be reliable and resourceful as well as reason
able in his charges for services rendered.
It is only after the itinerary receives final approval by the operat
ing and traffic departments that the actual choice of agents can be
made. In one port, the decision may be to use the branch office of a
liner-service company. Among the reasons influencing this deci-

## PASSENGER CRU ISES 407
sion might well be that the liner company is accustomed to handling
ships on fixed schedules, and has established certain routines to be
followed by its office staff. This kind of experience and "know how"
eliminates much of the chance that something may go wrong.
Then, too, the liner company is already established in the area, and
has the proper contacts and friends to dissolve difficulties if and
when they arise.
It is the responsibility of the operating manager to notify the
agent of his appointment. Full information on the ship's move
ments must be supplied, and the staff of the cruise ship must be
instructed when and how to communicate with him. The traffic
department also must be informed, so that it can list the names of
port agents for the information of passengers.
Employment aboard a cruise ship is very exacting, especially for
members of the catering staff who are in direct and constant contact
with the passengers. It is axiomatic that the master, purser, chief
steward, hotel manager (if there is one aboard), head waiter, and
senior wine steward be selected not only on the basis of technical
competence but also on their records for tact, diplomacy, suaveness
of manner, and patience. On the long cruises, these people, as well
as room stewards, bellboys, bartenders, and table waiters, are sub
jected to almost unending demands for special attention. They are
expected to render whatever service is asked promptly and courte
ously, regardless of how long they may have worked or the circum
stances in which this work has been performed. The senior medical
officer of the Queen Elizabeth 2 stated in an interview that he had
many patients from the steward's department who were suffering
from ulcers, hypertension, anxiety, and insomnia. In part these
health problems resulted from the length of the voyage, but the
principal reason was the constant striving always to please even the
most arbitrary and unreasonable passengers.
On the short voyages, some of the same problems exist among
shipboard personnel. While the ship completes a cruise in seven
days, a new cruise is scheduled to begin on the same day the ship
arrives in the home port. The eight to twelve hours in that port are
barely sufficient to accomplish the enormous amount of work re
quired. The ship must be cleaned thoroughly. Every cabin must be
stripped, wiped down, refitted, and made to look as though it never
had been used. Damaged fittings and scarred bulkheads must be

## 408 THE BUS I N E S S 0 F S HIP PIN G
repaired. Stores must be taken aboard and stowed properly. Deck
chairs must be inspected and replaced if necessary. The list is long,
and involves tedious and exhausting manual labor as well as alert
ness. Then, about two hours before sailing time, stewards must
make themselves ready to receive the new passengers. Because
many of the persons making the seven day cruises never have been
aboard ship before, or consider that having paid a comprehensive
fare they are going to "get their money's worth," the demands for
service may be anywhere from unending to degrading.
Selecting, training, supervising, and holding desirable and qual
ified seagoing personnel for cruise ships is a major responsibility of
shoreside management. This is being recognized by some opera
tors, who make special efforts to assure that working conditions are
the best possible in existing circumstances, and that personnel are
rotated between sea duty and shore leave in accordance with es
tablished schedules.
Although not a professional seafarer, and not in the chain of
shipboard command, the cruise director is important to the success
of every cruise. This person-there is no tradition that the director
be a man or a woman; both sexes have been employed with marked
success-is responsible for running all the shipboard games and
entertainments. Every activity is scheduled through the director's
office, so that no conflicts arise in the use of space or equipment.
Events involving passengers are listed by this office and published
in the ship's daily news sheet. To encourage cruise participants to
take part in those games, sports, meetings, sessions, or other forms
of amusements or instruction that take place during a typical day at
sea is the basic reason for having the director on board. The larger
ships have a director and several assistants, all of whom are qual
ified as recreation specialists.26 Administratively, the director and
staff are su pervised by the purser, but perform none of the techni
cal duties of that official.
Beside the cruise director, other "supernumeraries" in the
ship's complement include musicians, professional entertainers (of
ten hired directly from well-known nightclubs or variety shows, and
at the same compensation they receive for shoreside engagements),
lecturers, photographers, and in some ships a clergyman who
serves as the ship's chaplain. Lecturers and chaplains normally
travel as guests of the ship, exchanging their professional services
for the cruise.

## PASSENGER CRUISES 409
More and more, as other opportunities to experience the plea
sure of a sea voyage as part of international travel become less
available, cruises are gaining in popularity. Not only do they carry
the passenger to overseas points, but they offer a completely dif
ferent style of life for the duration of the voyage. As floating
vacations, cruises demand specialized management, and to meet
this demand corporations have been formed for the express pur
pose of designing, building, owning, and running ships that cater to
that fortunate group of people who find a way to make a cruise and
to enjoy the "thrill of a lifetime."27

## CHAPTER 20 - Industrial and Special Carriers
IN
C L U D ED in the thousands of vessels that collective
ly compose the mercantile marine of the world are many fleets
which, because of highly individualistic employment or very spe
cialized design, belong to one of two categories of shipping. The
first of these, the industrial carriers, serve the needs of particular
industrial enterprises, while the second, the special carriers, meet the
demands peculiar to certain types of cargo.
Industrial carriers are those ships that exist as part of the plant
and transportation properties oflarge industrial organizations and
are operated exclusively for them. The huge fleet of tankers main
tained by Exxon Corporation, the largest oil company in the world,
typifies this category. The only purpose of this globe-girdling ag
gregation of ships is to provide the transoceanic transportation
essential to the life of the parent concern. There are no other users
to be considered by the owners, and no obligations to be met except
those laid down by Exxon Corporation, which has full responsibil
ity and control.
Other ships, like the refrigerated fruit carriers or the auto
mobile transporters, are owned by steamship companies that are
not affiliated with any industrial activity but are built to carry a
certain cargo with greatest efficiency and economy. These ships are
the special carriers; they are made available, either under charter
or in regular berth service, to those users who must send their
goods across the oceans. For reasons of their own, these users do
not desire to become owners and operators of ships and are satis
fied to benefit from the capability without having to accept the
responsibilities and burdens of ownership.
It is apparent that many ships appropriately designated as in
dustrial carriers are designed to transport a single commodity and
therefore may be considered as both special and industrial carriers.

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 411
There is nothing contradictory in this finding, so long as the princi
ples underlying the reasons for the existence of these ships are
understood.
INDUSTRIAL CARRIERS
The industrial carrier is defined as the marine transpor
tation link in the process of manufacture or distribution (or both) of
the materials used or produced by an industrial organization. Some
carriers handle nothing but raw materials, and others transport
only the finished goods, while still others may be used
interchangeably.
It must be pointed out that some industrial carriers serve a
one-way trade, and as a consequence the parent concern may place
the ship in berth service, or on voyage charter, for the return trip.
The economic reason for this action is to reduce the cost of ship
operation, and to eliminate, if possible, the need to sail ships in
ballast on nonrevenue producing voyages.
The Broken Hill Proprietary, of Australia, operates a fleet of
eighteen bulk carriers and two specialized roll-on/roll-off steel car
riers. The bulkers haul raw materials to company steelworks; occa
sionally they are dispatched to Southeast Asia. The roll-on/roll-off
ships move semifinished and finished steel products from plants to
customers in Australia and across the Tasman Sea. An understand
ing of the magnitude of this operation is derived from the fact that
the annual cost thereof exceeds one hundred and fifty million
dollars.l
Crown-Zellerbach Corporation, the paper manufacturing and
distributing organization based in San Francisco, California, has
two specialized ships under long-term charter to haul newsprint
from Scandinavia and Canada to California ports. Serving only the
charterers, these ships are classified correctly as industrial carriers.
Exxon Corporation, already mentioned, at the end of 1984
operated a fleet of 104 ships of its own and about fifty additional
units chartered from other sources. Ranging in size from 3,800 tons
to 516,423 tons deadweight, this is one of the largest tanker fleets in
the world. A complete vessel operating department exists within
the Exxon corporate structure to control and administer this fleet.
United Brands, the banana-growing and distributing company
domiciled in the United States, owns a fleet of eleven refrigerated

## 412 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
ships for the sole purpose of providing the transportation required
to serve its markets.
The principal purposes served by the industrial carrier are:
1. To control delivery schedules of raw materials or finished
products (or both) handled by the parent concern.
2. To reduce the cost of ocean transportation by elimination of
the "middleman's" profit.
3. To provide efficient transportation to the destinations re
quired by the parent company for the type of cargo of particular
interest to it.
There is a heavy burden of expense and responsibility inherent
in the ownership and operation of a fleet of oceangoing vessels.
Only those corporations with a continuing need for transportation
of goods in shipload lots, or between ports not served by common
carriers, have found it profitable to establish and maintain indus
trial carrier service. The large investment of capital may deter some
companies from buying their own ships; instead, they resort to
-
,.....
A lumber carrier, Star Columbia depends upon her own gantry cranes for
fast dispatch. Photo: Munck International.

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 413
Top left: Mel-(lin H. Baker, a self-unloading bulk carrier. The port for the
conveyor is at the stern. Top right: Discharging conveyor extended
through stern port. Bottom left: Discharging coal to inland waterways
barges. Bottom right: Conveyor systems below cargo compartments. Pho-
tos: Nordstroms Marine Systems.
long-term chartering. It is the use to which the ship is put, rather
than the design or the ownership, which determines the classifica
tion of a vessel as an industrial carrier.
It is worth repeating that industrial carriers may be of a special
ized design, but only when this suits the needs of the industry they
serve. The fleet of Broken Hill Proprietary is an excellent exponent
of this principle. The roll-on/roll-off ships were designed for the
express purpose of transporting the long lengths and prefabricated
pieces of steel sold by the parent company to purchasers in differ
ent parts of Australia. No other ships could perform this task so well
or so economically.

## 414 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The Rondeggen carried rolls of newsprint in her holds and packaged
lumber on deck. Three traveling gantry cranes assured fast cargo han
dling. Photo: Brenner, courtesy Crown Zellerbach.
Considerations of cost also may influence a company initially to
charter one or more ships of either conventional or specialized
type, depending upon what is required and what is available on the
market that is satisfactory for the intended purpose. As experience
is gained, and the economics of the situation may justify, ships of
appropriately specialized character may be acquired.
It has been noted above that one advantage of the industrial
carrier is that it gives to the parent company control over delivery
schedules. Vessels are dispatched in accordance with the needs of
the industry; there is no concern for possible customers who might
be upset by sudden changes in sailing times, ports of call, or space
allocations within the ship. To companies like Exxon, United
Brands, Crown Zellerbach, and Broken Hill, which must depend
upon ships to bring in materials indispensable to their operations,
this factor alone justifies the maintenance of controlled fleets. The
fruit company also has flexibility in routing its ships to those ports
where the demand for bananas is at the maximum. In this way, it
satisfies the demands of its retail merchant-customers as well as
capitalizing on opportunities to sell the bananas at the highest
pnces.
Two other advantages accrue to the owner of an industrial
carrier. One is the lower cost arising from elimination of the profit
seeking shipping organization, while the other is the independence
of the ship charter market conferred upon the parent industry by
having a fleet already under its control. The aggregate savings from

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 415
Gantry crane with specialized lifting device and clear plastic overhead
panels handles newsprint safely in any weather. Photo: Munck
International.
these factors, tangible and otherwise, often outweigh the cost of
owning and managing the fleet.
Finally, it was noted that an advantage to the parent concern of
the industrial carrier is that it provides efficient and careful trans
portation for the proprietary cargoes. As experience is gained by
the operator of an industrial carrier in handling one or more
commodities, many cost-saving and profit-producing techniques

## 416 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The diesel-powered ore-bulk-oil carrier Furness Bridge (built 1971) had a
deadweight of 166,064 tons and a speed of 15.5 knots. Piping for oil
cargoes lies outboard ofthe hatches with their thwartships sliding covers.
Photo: Ralston, courtesy Furness Withy Group.

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 417
are certain to be developed, eventually culminating in highly effec
tive, efficient, and economical methods.
For these reasons, industrial carriers are justified. The princi
ples are not violated by using chartered ships, so long as the charter
er maintains the same complete control over the ships' activities as if
he had title to those vessels.
SPECIALIZED CARRIERS
One of the more interesting, and certainly significant,
trends in mercantile shipping during the twentieth century has
been the development of specialized carriers, which are found
today on practically all sea routes. Their numbers and variety
increase annually, and bear witness to their ever growing
importance.
The specialized ship is one designed for the express purpose of
carrying, usually in shipload lots, a particular type of cargo, either
in bulk or in containers of stipulated size. A specialized ship thus
may be constructed for the exclusive carriage of alumina; a com
pletely different design would be needed for a vessel hauling auto
mobiles and trucks.
Among the many types of specialized carriers are ships built to
transport bauxite, bulk cement, chemicals in liquid form, coal, iron
ore, liquefied natural gas, newsprint and other paper products,
petroleum and its by-products, refrigerated fruits and meats, wood
chips, and wood pulp.
The significant feature, by which a ship employed in this man
ner is assigned to the category of a specialized ship, is the design, as
distinguished from the industrial carrier, which is classified accord
ing to the use to which it is put.
Although transportation by water is the most economical means
of movement ever devised by man, there is no single commodity for
which the individual cost of carriage does not constitute, in some
measure, a barrier to its ready sale in the markets of the world. The
carrier is entitled to be compensated for his services, but the ship
per demands that this compensation be kept at the lowest possible
level. To satisfy this requirement has called for the exercise of
amazing ingenuity on the part of the shipowner, the naval architect,
and the shipbuilder. Particularly in the second half of the twentieth

## 418 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
The starkly utilitarian design of the Japanese automobile/truck carrier
Nada V is her dominant characteristic. Photo: Moran Towing Co.
century have the fruits of these endeavors become important in a
wide range of shipping operations, as one specialized design after
another has been created to transport commodities once consid
ered immovable, or to reduce the costs of carriage to levels previ
ously believed not to be feasible. Extraordinarily complex ships
have been built to provide safe and efficient, as well as economical,
transportation. Paralleling these achievements, and often contrib
uting to the success of the project, have been the successful attacks
on that age-old problem of the shipowner: to reduce the time
required to work the ship in port and send her back to sea.
Designed as she is to transport most efficiently a single commod
ity in shipload lots, the specialized carrier often succeeds in areas
where the conventional ship would be totally unsatisfactory. An
interesting demonstration of this thesis may be found in the trans
oceanic movement of liquefied natural and petroleum gas. Efforts
to transport these by-products of the oil fields which were then, and
still are, in great demand, began in the 1950s, but it was not until
1960 that a commercially practicable method of handling these

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 419
cargoes was brought to perfection. The first full scale experiments
involved the shipment of methane, which becomes liquid when
cooled to -162° Celsius (-265° Fahrenheit). One of the many
distinctive features of liquefied gas, insofar as its carriage by ship is
concerned, is its bulkiness; one naval architect estimated that it
occupied about four times the space of an equivalent tonnage of
petroleum. Special ballast tanks to bring the ship down to her
normal draft were needed.2
A small dry-cargo ship was converted into a liquid methane
carrier in 1958. The Methane Pioneer used a "wool" made from balsa
wood as the principal insulation for the cargo tanks. The experi
ment was so successful that about two years later the Bridgestone
Maru, a tanker of 25,626 tons deadweight, was built particularly to
haul liquefied gas. She was assigned to the supply route from
Indonesia to Japan. Her performance validated many theories
relating to the technique of moving liquefied gases.3
As experience was gained with the pioneering ships, significant
advances were made in the design, metallurgy, and insulation of the
tanks in which these cargoes were carried. Beginning in 1962, a
succession of improved and enlarged gas carriers was put into
service. As if to prove that the movement of liquefied gas could
become an entirely routine and uneventful activity, the Polar Alaska
and the Arctic Tokyo, each with a capacity of 71,645 cubic meters of
liquid gas, completed more than 110 round trips between Alaska
and Yokohama in the six years that ended on May 31, 1976.4
In September 1975, the 125, OOO-cubic meter E l Paso Paul Kayser
was delivered by her builders, Chantiers de France - Dunkerque.
This twenty-knot, turbine-driven vessel was 280.62 meters long,
41.60 meters in beam, and had a draft of 11 meters. The six cargo
tanks were encased with heavy insulation to hold the cargo at -165°
Celsius. Boilers supplying steam to the 45,000-horsepower turbines
were equipped not only to burn oil but also to accept the gas that
"boiled off' from the cargo. To provide maximum safety, each
cargo tank was isolated by cofferdams at the forward and after
ends, by wing tanks on each side, and by a double bottom. The wing
tanks could be used for ballast water, and provision was made to
heat this water in the event of cargo leakage to prevent the exces
sively cold gas from damaging the intrinsic properties of the steel in
the hull structure.5

## 420 THE B USIN ESS OF SH IPPI NG
The packaged-lumber carrier Emma Johanna was equipped with six cen
terline cranes to assure rapid cargo-handling. Photo: Jeff Blinn, courtesy
Moran Towing Co.
Considered to be among the most complex of ship designs,
these liquefied natural gas carriers were enormously expensive to
build. The contracts for the first vessels of this type to be built in the
United States were signed in 1972, and the price was just over 100
million dollars for each of three. By the fall of 1980, the price had
risen to approximately 155 million dollars. There was sufficient
demand for the gas, however, to justify construction worldwide of a
fleet which, at the end of 1980, numbered 65 liquefied natural gas
carriers of 5,400,000 cubic meters total capacity, and 568 liquefied
petroleum gas carriers, with an aggregate of 6,500,000 cubic me
ters of cargo tank space.
An example, although somewhat old, of developing a special
type of ship to meet the peculiar demands of an industry was
presented in 1956 with the entry into service of the Duncan Bay.
This 20,000-ton deadweight capacity tanker was built to carry wood
pulp which had been loaded in a wet state (approximately one-third
pulp to two-thirds water), and after the water was pumped out of
the tanks, was transported from British Columbia to California.
There the pulp was resaturated to a consistency of 98 percent water

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 421
To assure uncontaminated handling of many different types of chemicals
at one time, an intricate web of cargo pipes is essential. Photo: Moss
Rosenberg Shipbuilders.
and 2 percent pulp, so that it could be pumped into "desaturation"
tanks before being stored. The ultimate paper product was im
proved through elimination of the process by which the pulp was
dried at the mill before shipping. At the same time, the cost of
handling was reduced by the mechanized loading and discharging
arrangement. Special features incorporated into the ship's design
were turbine-driven pumps for the dilution water, agitators to
blend and keep the pulp in solution during the unloading opera
tion, and nine high capacity pumps to transfer the pulp from the
twelve cargo tanks.6
In the construction and long-term charter of the Norwegian
motors hips Besseggen and Rondeggen are seen examples of the pro
cedure by which an industrial shipper will obtain the use of a
specialized ship without investing a substantial amount of his own
capital or establishing a marine department to operate the ships.
Crown Zellerbach Company wanted a ship to carry 5,500 tons of

## 422 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
This modern chemical tanker has thirty-five piping arrangements. Four
deck-mounted tanks accommodate small lots of specialized cargo. Photo:
Stolt-Nielsen, Inc.
newsprint in rolls stowed below decks and 1,000 tons of packaged
lumber on deck. High-speed loading and discharging in any kind
of weather was required. A Norwegian shipowner agreed to build
the ships with the stipulation that they be time chartered for a major
part of the anticipated life of the vessels.
Acting in concert, the charterer, owner, designer, and builder
produced a noteworthy solution to the problem. The engine and
bridge were located at the after end of the ship, to provide the deck
space for the packaged lumber and to facilitate operation of three
gantry cranes installed on rails extending the length of the weather
deck. The cranes were equipped with hydraulically operated,
hinged jibs to provide long outreach over the ship's sides when
loading or discharging. A rack-and-pinion driven trolley traveled
athwartships on rails welded to the main girders and jibs, and could
function despite noticeable list or tilt of the ship. A permanent,

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 423
The chemical tanker has a complex piping system, double bottom, double
skin, and cofferdams separating the numerous cargo tanks. Photo: Stolt
Nielsen, Inc.
semitranslucc::nt weather cover over the main girders provided the
mandatory protection against rain and snow, and the sides of the
cranes had curtains to complete the "all weather" protection. The
cranes weighed 126 tons each, and had a lifting capacity of 30,000
pounds. Special yokes were devised to accommodate eight rolls of
newsprint simultaneously, and another attachment was developed
to handle the lumber. The cyclic rate of cargo work was 1,000 tons
per hour; experience showed that about 750 tons per hour rep
resented the practical maximum. A minimum number of long
shoremen was required for the cargo-handling operation.
These two highly specialized ships proved to be both economi
cal and efficient. Much of their success could be attributed to the
cranes, which were of unique design and utility, very heavy, and
quite expensive. It was reported that they cost $196,000 apiece
when the ships were delivered at the end of 1962; an investment

## 424 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
which would make the general service carrier look critically at any
benefits which were expected to redound to him?
In many parts of the world, there is a continuing demand for
cement in very large quantities. For decades, it was impossible to
ship this commodity in bulk and to discharge it with the self-unload
ing equipment installed in coal and ore carriers. Cement is ex
tremely fine and powdery in its composition, packs easily, and has a
very high angle of repose. Mechanical discharge required that the
cement flow to the conveyors, a movement eventually achieved by
pumping compressed air into the mass. Using this principle, en
gineers developed several self-unloading mechanisms by which
ships carrying cement in bulk could discharge their cargoes effi
ciently and economically.
When she was delivered to her owners in 1981, the Castillo de
Javier was the largest self-unloading bulk cement carrier in the
world. She was a vessel of 44,000 metric tons deadweight, and had
six holds. Two of these holds were exceptionally large and were
intended for the cement cargoes. Transverse screw conveyors
moved the cargo to each side of the hold where the cement fell
through valve-operated trap doors into pipeways laid in the double
bottoms. Vacuum pumps sucked the cement out of the ship in two
cycles, one from the hold to a reloader chamber, and the other from
that chamber to the shoreside silo. By means of this system, the ship
could be emptied at the rate of 600 tons an hour. The builders
claimed that the mechanism they devised made it possible to carry
not only bulk cement but also low density commodities like flour or
any granular or pulverized material. A further advantage of the
pipeway system was that the tank tops were flat and easy to clean,
thereby making it feasible to carry different types of cargo on the
return voyage. The ship's utility was enhanced by the three twenty
ton cranes so mounted that they served all six hatches.8
There is a significant movement to all parts of the world of
unusually large or extremely heavy articles, of which railroad loco
motives and electrical generator components are typical, which
cannot be transported in the cargo liners and tramps used in
general trade. To meet this demand, some steamship operators
designed their liner-service ships so that they had the necessary
heavy-lift capability, their decks would support the weight, and the
hatches were long enough and wide enough to permit these over
size items to be stowed under cover.

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 425
The specialized heavy-lift motorship UhenfeL5 uses both her 275-ton
booms to hoist a SOO-ton heat exchanger. Photo: Hansa Line.
Typical of the cargo liners with high capacity booms was the
Benarty, of the Ben Line, an English company serving the routes
from the United Kingdom to the Far East. She was fitted with a
Stuelcken derrick of 180 tons capacity which was mounted between
two hatches 12.19 and 15.92 meters long, respectively. Until its
liquidation in 1980, the German shipping company, Hansa Linie,
for many years operated a fleet of cargo liners with Stuelcken
booms of 350 tons which could be paired to lift a total of 700 tons.
American Heavy Lift Shipping Company, of Pittsburgh, Pennsyl-

## 426 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
vania, owned the only two purpose-built heavy-lift ships to fly the
United States flag. These vessels, the John Henry and Paul Bunyan,
each had two 216-ton cranes, bow and stern ramps strong enough
to accept weights of 1,000 tons, and a shallow draft of 5.15 meters.
They were employed in worldwide tramp-type serviceY
One of the reasons that cargo liner operators put specialized
ships into their services is that it assures them of a segment of the
business which, but for this capability, would be denied to them
altogether. Often the special capability applies to cargo moving
only in one direction, as in the case of meats, bananas, and other
perishable fruits, and vegetables brought to consuming countries in
ever increasing quantities. On the return passage, these specialized
carriers offer to carry any kind of general cargo that will not
damage the expensive equipment of the vessel or interfere unduly
with the schedules of the homebound perishables. It must be noted,
in passing, that the stipulation by the owner or operator that only
the specialized cargo will be accepted does not change the common
carrier status of the line.
A large fleet of refrigerated ships has been built since the end of
World War II, both by liner companies and by independent owners
who seek long-term charters from importers of refrigerated goods.
Most of the independent (tramp) "reefers" are owned by Euro
pean, and particularly by Scandinavian, operators. The liner com
panies running reefers are of many nationalities and are found on
numerous trade routes.
A further example of the responsiveness of shipowners to de
mands for specialized tonnage may be found in the automobile
carriers operated from Europe and Japan to the United States. A
considerable variation between designs manifested itself; some
ships, like the Wallenius Lines' Madame Butterfly, were designated as
"pure" vehicle carriers, because they did not attempt to carry bulk
cargoes on the return passage. Other ships were equipped with
portable 'tween decks which could be stowed under the main deck
to permit full use of the holds for loads of grain. Most of these
vehicle ships were designed with side or stern ports for drive-on
loading and drive-off discharge. 10
Sea-carriage always has been responsive to the needs of ship
pers, and the development of the numerous types of specialized
carriers during the twentieth century merely emphasizes this char-

## INDUSTRIAL AND SPECIAL CARRIERS 427
acteristic of the shipping industry. The only limits to the possibili
ties appear to be the imaginations of the users and the naval ar
chitects. Only a few of the specialized designs have been mentioned
in this chapter because of the wide range of the subject. Economic
reasons of the strongest sort have undergirded the construction
and operation of ships with unique capabilities to support a particu
lar industry. Equally valid have been the justifications found by
common carriers to tailor their ships to fit a certain subdivision of
the trade routes they serve. As has been pointed out, economic
demands often result in a merging of industrial and specialized
carriers, and it is important to recall that it is the manner in which
the capabilities of the ship are held out to the user that determines
whether a ship properly may be assigned to one category or the
other.

## CHAPTER 21 - TankerA1anagement
RANGING in size from enormous to almost tiny,
transporting more tons of cargo of direct concern to more people
in more parts of the world than any other type of ship afloat,
tankers have become indispensable to the modern international
industrial economy. As vital components of the merchant marine,
tankers require many of the same managerial skills needed by any
other type of ships engaged in moving cargoes across the seas.
Dedicated to the movement of liquid commodities in bulk, how
ever, these vessels demand specialized knowledge and techniques
both from the personnel who take them to sea and those on shore
who supervise their activities.
In the age-old history of shipping, transporting oil in bulk is a
relatively new enterprise. The first steamship designed and built to
carry petroleum in bulk, rather than in wooden barrels or metal
drums was put into service in 1886.1 This was the Glilckauf ("Good
Luck"), built in England to the order of Wilhelm A. Riedemann, a
merchant of Hamburg, to serve the German-American Petroleum
Company.2 She was a small ship, 300 feet long with a deadweight of
3,020 tons-hardly noticeable alongside today's gigantic crude oil
carriers. Her place in history, however, derives from the fact that
she was the prototype of the world's tankers, with steam driven
pumps segregated from the cargo tanks by steel bulkheads, and
engine-room at the after end, separated from the cargo tank area
by a cofferdam formed by twin bulkheads. This pattern of con
struction, it is appropriate to note, has not altered significantly over
the years.
I wish to express my appreciation for the assistance received from Messrs. Robert
F. Matthes, Carroll Bjornson, and Paul de Gregorio, of Stolt-Nielsen Inc., and
Captain John H. Merrell, of Texaco, Inc., who read the manuscript of this chapter
and provided much valuable information.

## TANKER MANAGEMENT 429
Since the GlUckauf was launched, bulk oil carriers have multi
plied in numbers, increased in speed and, most conspicuously,
grown enormously in size. Change was slow at first; as late as 1920,
the standard tanker had a deadweight of only some 12,000 tons,
although there were a few vessels of about 20,000 tons.3 In 1939, a
small group of 16,000-tonners was in operation, but the majority of
tanker-owning companies still leaned toward the "handy-sized"
ships of 12,000 tons. It is a minor footnote to history that the
average speed of all these ships was about eleven knots, compared
to the GlUckaufs ten.
The demands for transportation of petroleum during World
War II greatly exceeded the capability of the prewar fleet and called
forth new standards for tankers. Bigger and faster ships were
essential, and the United States built 532 oil carriers of the T-2 class.
With a deadweight of about 16,750 tons and a speed of 14.5 knots,
these ships proved incontrovertibly that large, fast oil carriers were
economical, efficient, and as versatile as the handy-sized tankers of
12,000 tons.4
Once the utility of the T-2 had been demonstrated, growth
became characteristic of the tanker-operating industry. In 1947,
the first of the really big ships were built. These "colossal" vessels of
25,000 to 27,000 tons deadweight were relegated quickly to lesser
positions by the prompt advent of tankers of 32,000 tons and, very
shortly thereafter, of 45,000 tons. By 1979, the peak had been
reached with the completion of the Seawise Giant, a crude oil carrier
of 569,783 tons deadweight owned by the C. Y. Tung interests of
Hong Kong. Owing to a reduction in consumption of oil world
wide, the demand for the very large crude carriers decreased
markedly, and no building programs including ships as large as the
Seawise Giant have been initiated since then.5
Paralleling the enlargement of the ship hulls was the prolifera
tion of tankers in the world's merchant fleets. On January 1, 1984,
there were in existence 5,548 tankers, including: 74 specialized
vessels designed to carry asphalt, molasses, wine, and other unusual
liquid cargoes; 752 chemical transporters; 4 7 liquefied natural gas
carriers; and 448 liquefied petroleum gas carriers.6 The big car
riers (generally those of 200,000 tons deadweight or larger) are
used exclusively to move raw petroleum from the oil fields to
refineries. Distribution of refined products traditionally has been

## 430 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
'.
Top: The 14.5-knot, 16,650-tons deadweight T-2 tanker proved her worth
in war and peace. Bottom: First of the American-flag supertankers, Esso
Zurich (built 1949) had a deadweight of 26,555 tons, and was 628 feet
long. Photo: Standard Oil Co.
the province of the smaller ships. The considerations limiting size
have been related to depth of approach channels and restricted
storage facilities ashore. As a result, a sizeable fleet of tankers of
3,500 to 10,000 tons deadweight was employed in this service. In
recent years, however, to take advantage of the economies inherent
in larger ships, and to capitalize on advances in naval architecture,
tankers up to 42,000 tons deadweight, along with a few broad-

## TANKER MANAGEMENT 431
beamed, shallow-draft petroleum carriers of nearly 80,000 tons
deadweight capacity, have been built for these trades.
As the size of ships increased, tanker owners showed more
interest in obtaining optimum performance, but had to wait for the
acceptance of computers as standard tools of management before
realizing significant progress. To illustrate how the capabilities of
the computer might be used by an operator, a study was conducted
in 1970 of the hypothetical employment under time charter of a
20-knot, 500,000-tons deadweight tanker making a round voyage
from the Arabian Gulf to Northwest Europe, a total distance of
22,614 miles. Fuel consumption for the big ship varied from 200
tons per day at a cruising speed of 14 knots to 790 tons per day at
the maximum of 20 knots. The cost of fuel oil, at the time the
computation was made, was $17.50 per ton. At the slowest speed,
the round voyage would take 67 days and would require 13,460
tons of fuel. At maximum speed, twenty days would be saved, but
fuel consumption would jump to 37,209 tons. Calculating the pos
sibilities for different cruising speeds, the computer showed that
ordering the ship to travel at 16.5 knots for the round voyage of 57
days, with a total consumption of 20,355 tons of oil would be the
most effective employment of the tanker. Assuming that the ship
were registered under a flag of convenience and had a crew paid
minimum wages, the owner could cover all expenses and have a
profit equal to 10 percent on the investment if charter hire were
fixed at $1.15 per deadweight ton per month. This equated to a
freight rate of $3.05 per ton of oil delivered.7
During the years that the number and size of tankers in the
world fleet was increasing, a concurrent change in the pattern of
ownership became evident. Shordy after the end of World War II
in 1945, half of the world's tanker tonnage was owned and operated
by the oil companies. Three decades later, operators not affiliated
with the petroleum producing and refining corporations (and
therefore usually designated as the "independents") controlled
nearly 75 percent of total tanker tonnage.8 In the five years which
ended in June 1983, the major oil companies (BP, Chevron, Exxon,
Gulf, Mobil, Royal Dutch Shell, and Texaco) reduced their fleets by
40 percent, compared to a worldwide decline in tanker tonnage of
10 percent. During that same half-decade these seven companies
cut transportation in company vessels from about 37 percent to 23

## 432 THE BUSINESS OF SHIPPING
Top: The 32,650-tons deadweight Cities SenJice Baltimore (built 1956) car
ried 18 grades of petroleum products. Bottom: The 16-knot Sealift Arctic,
on a draft of 10.7 meters, has a deadweight of 27,225 tons, and can enter
almost any harbor. Photo: Jeff Blinn, courtesy Moran Towing Co.
percentY This shift in capability was deliberate, as the producers
sought both to allocate to different purposes the funds invested in
tankers and to divest themselves of the burdens of fleet ownership.
There has been no diminution of interest on the part of the oil
companies in the capabilities and economics of tankers, whether

## TANKER MANAGEMENT 433
owned or chartered. Pressure on the operating and marine traffic
departments, which are concerned direcdy with the ships, remains
as great as ever. Indeed, there is a good argument that the pressure
is greater as the duration of voyages has been shortened by higher
sea speeds and concurrent reduction in port turnaround time
resulting from the capabilities of ships' pumps to discharge their
cargoes in fewer hours. There is, consequendy, less time to accom
plish the many duties involved in transporting oil across the seas.
Also, the availability of adequate storage tanks ashore to accommo
date the huge quantities of oil delivered by the big carriers is much
more of a problem than in earlier days. Operating expenses of
modern tankers are extremely high, making every minute the ship
is not moving between ports a matter of concern. Refineries set
their schedules on the assumption that tanker deliveries will not be
delayed for any reason, thereby augmenting the pressure on the
operating department to assure that vessels, both owned and char
tered, are in peak condition.
